text:
Doubt me, my dim companion!
Why, God would be content
With but a fraction of the love
Poured thee without a stint.
The whole of me, forever,
What more the woman can, --
Say quick, that I may dower thee
With last delight I own!

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Doubt me, my shadowed fellow!
Why, God might well be pleased
With but a shard of the love
Outpoured on thee unmeasured.
The whole of me, forever,—
What more can woman give?—
Speak swiftly, that I may endow thee
With the last delight I keep!
source_text:
Doubt me, my dim companion!
Why, God would be content
With but a fraction of the love
Poured thee without a stint.
The whole of me, forever,
What more the woman can, --
Say quick, that I may dower thee
With last delight I own!

text:
The Hero of these artless strains,
A lowly bard was he,
Who sung his rhymes in Coila’s plains,
With meikle mirth an’glee;
Kind Nature’s care had given his share
Large, of the flaming current;
And, all devout, he never sought
To stem the sacred torrent.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
The Hero of these artless lays,  
A humble bard was he,  
Who sang his rhymes in Coila’s plains,  
With meikle mirth an’ glee;  
Kind Nature’s care had dealt his share  
Full large of the flaming current;  
And, all devout, he never sought  
To stem the sacred torrent.

A lowly bard was he,
Who sung his rhymes in Coila’s plains,
With meikle mirth an’glee;
Kind Nature’s care had given his share
Large, of the flaming current;
And, all devout, he never sought
To stem the sacred torrent.

text:
Who telleth a tale of unspeaking death?
Who lifteth the veil of what is to come?
Who painteth the shadows that are beneath
The wide-winding caves of the peopled tomb?
Or uniteth the hopes of what shall be
With the fears and the love for that which we see?

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
What chemistry!
That the winds are not truly contagious,
That this is no deception, this clear green-wash of the sea, which
is so longing after me,
That it is safe to let it lick my bare body everywhere with its tongues,
That it will not threaten me with the fevers that have settled
within it,
That all remains clean, forever and ever,
That the cool draught from the well tastes so sweet,
That blackberries are so rich and juicy,
That the fruits of the apple-grove and the orange-grove, that
melons, grapes, peaches, plums, none of them will poison me,
That when I lie back on the grass I do not contract any illness,
Though perhaps every blade of grass springs from what was once
an illness that could be caught.
source_text:
What chemistry!
That the winds are really not infectious,
That this is no cheat, this transparent green-wash of the sea which
is so amorous after me,
That it is safe to allow it to lick my naked body all over with its tongues,
That it will not endanger me with the fevers that have deposited
themselves in it,
That all is clean forever and forever,
That the cool drink from the well tastes so good,
That blackberries are so flavorous and juicy,
That the fruits of the apple-orchard and the orange-orchard, that
melons, grapes, peaches, plums, will none of them poison me,
That when I recline on the grass I do not catch any disease,
Though probably every spear of grass rises out of what was once
catching disease.

text:
Morn fled, noon came, evening, then night descended,
And we prolonged calm talk beneath the sphere
Of the calm moon—when suddenly was blended
With our repose a nameless sense of fear;
And from the cave behind I seemed to hear
Sounds gathering upwards!—accents incomplete,
And stifled shrieks,—and now, more near and near,
A tumult and a rush of thronging feet
The cavern’s secret depths beneath the earth did beat.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Morn passed away, then noon, then evening, and at last night fell,  
And we prolonged our tranquil talk beneath the sphere  
Of the still moon—when all at once was mingled  
With our repose a dim and nameless fear;  
And from the cave behind I seemed to hear  
Voices ascending!—broken, imperfect sounds,  
And smothered shrieks,—and now, more near, more near,  
A tumult and a trampling of crowded feet  
That beat through the secret hollows of the earth beneath.
source_text:
Morn fled, noon came, evening, then night descended,
And we prolonged calm talk beneath the sphere
Of the calm moon—when suddenly was blended
With our repose a nameless sense of fear;
And from the cave behind I seemed to hear
Sounds gathering upwards!—accents incomplete,
And stifled shrieks,—and now, more near and near,
A tumult and a rush of thronging feet
The cavern’s secret depths beneath the earth did beat.

text:
A few quadrillions of eras, a few octillions of cubic leagues, do
not hazard the span or make it impatient,
They are but parts, any thing is but a part.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
A few quadrillions of eras, a few octillions of cubic leagues, do  
not endanger the reach nor render it impatient,  
They are but fragments, every thing is but a fragment.

text:
The water-blooms under the rivulet
Fell from the stalks on which they were set;
And the eddies drove them here and there,
As the winds did those of the upper air.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
The water-blooms beneath the little stream  
Dropped from the stalks where erst they used to gleam;  
And the whirling eddies bore them here and there,  
As winds had done with those in upper air.
source_text:
The water-blooms under the rivulet
Fell from the stalks on which they were set;
And the eddies drove them here and there,
As the winds did those of the upper air.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Some keep the Sabbath by going to church;
I keep it by remaining home,
With a bobolink as choirmaster,
And an orchard serving as a dome.
source_text:
Some keep the Sabbath going to church;
I keep it staying at home,
With a bobolink for a chorister,
And an orchard for a dome.

text:
Bards of Passion and of Mirth,
Ye have left your souls on earth!
Have ye souls in heaven too,
Double-lived in regions new?
Yes, and those of heaven commune
With the spheres of sun and moon;
With the noise of fountains wond'rous,
And the parle of voices thund'rous;
With the whisper of heaven's trees
And one another, in soft ease
Seated on Elysian lawns
Brows'd by none but Dian's fawns
Underneath large blue-bells tented,
Where the daisies are rose-scented,
And the rose herself has got
Perfume which on earth is not;
Where the nightingale doth sing
Not a senseless, tranced thing,
But divine melodious truth;
Philosophic numbers smooth;
Tales and golden histories
Of heaven and its mysteries.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Bards of Passion and of Mirth,  
Ye have left your souls on earth!  
Have ye souls in heaven too,  
Double-liv’d in realms anew?  
Ay, and those of heaven converse  
With the spheres of sun and moon;  
With the murmur of fountains wondrous,  
And the parley of voices thunderous;  
With the whisper of heaven’s trees  
And one another, in soft ease  
Seated on Elysian lawns  
Browsed by none but Dian’s fawns  
Beneath wide blue-bells canopied,  
Where the daisies breathe of rose,  
And the rose herself hath won  
Perfume unknown beneath the sun;  
Where the nightingale doth sing  
Not a void, enchanted thing,  
But divine melodious truth;  
Philosophic numbers smooth;  
Tales and golden histories  
Of heaven and its mysteries.
source_text:

Yes, and those of heaven commune

With the noise of fountains wond'rous,
And the parle of voices thund'rous;
With the whisper of heaven's trees
And one another, in soft ease
Seated on Elysian lawns
Brows'd by none but Dian's fawns
Underneath large blue-bells tented,
Where the daisies are rose-scented,
And the rose herself has got
Perfume which on earth is not;
Where the nightingale doth sing
Not a senseless, tranced thing,
But divine melodious truth;
Philosophic numbers smooth;
Tales and golden histories
Of heaven and its mysteries.

text:
More popular poets clothe the ideal with familiar and sensible
imagery. Shelley loved to idealize the real—to gift the mechanism of
the material universe with a soul and a voice, and to bestow such also
on the most delicate and abstract emotions and thoughts of the mind.
Sophocles was his great master in this species of imagery.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
I call no Goddess to inspire my strains,
A fabled Muse may suit a bard that feigns:
Friend of my life! my ardent spirit burns,
And all the tribute of my heart returns,
For boons accorded, goodness ever new,
The gifts still dearer, as the giver you.
Thou orb of day! thou other paler light!
And all ye many sparkling stars of night!
If aught that giver from my mind efface,
If I that giver’s bounty e’er disgrace,
Then roll to me along your wand’rig spheres,
Only to number out a villain’s years!
I lay my hand upon my swelling breast,
And grateful would, but cannot speak the rest.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
I call no Goddess forth to fire my strains,  
A feigned Muse may suit the bard who feigns:  
Friend of my life! my fervent spirit burns,  
And all the tribute of my heart returns,  
For boons bestowed, for goodness ever new,  
The gifts yet dearer, as they came from you.  
Thou orb of day! thou other, paler light!  
And all ye myriad sparkling stars of night!  
If aught that giver from my mind efface,  
If I that giver’s bounty e’er disgrace,  
Then roll to me along your wand’ring spheres,  
Only to number out a villain’s years!  
I lay my hand upon my swelling breast,  
And grateful would, but cannot speak the rest.

A fabled Muse may suit a bard that feigns:
Friend of my life! my ardent spirit burns,
And all the tribute of my heart returns,
For boons accorded, goodness ever new,
The gifts still dearer, as the giver you.
Thou orb of day! thou other paler light!
And all ye many sparkling stars of night!
If aught that giver from my mind efface,
If I that giver’s bounty e’er disgrace,
Then roll to me along your wand’rig spheres,
Only to number out a villain’s years!
I lay my hand upon my swelling breast,
And grateful would, but cannot speak the rest.

text:
Farewell to the mountains, high-cover’d with snow,
Farewell to the straths and green vallies below;
Farewell to the forests and wild-hanging woods,
Farewell to the torrents and loud-pouring floods.
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Farewell to the mountains, with snow on their head,  
Farewell to the straths and green valleys below spread;  
Farewell to the forests and wild-drooping woods,  
Farewell to the torrents and loud-rushing floods.  
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.
source_text:

Farewell to the forests and wild-hanging woods,

text:
Chorus—I’ll aye ca’ in by yon town,
And by yon garden-green again;
I’ll aye ca’ in by yon town,
And see my bonie Jean again.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
The snowdrop, and then the violet,
Arose from the ground with warm rain wet,
And their breath was mixed with fresh odour, sent
From the turf, like the voice and the instrument.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
I'll tell you how the sun rose, --
A ribbon at a time.
The steeples swam in amethyst,
The news like squirrels ran.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
I'll tell you how the sun arose, --
In ribbons, one by one.
The steeples floated in amethyst,
The tidings like squirrels ran.
source_text:
I'll tell you how the sun rose, --
A ribbon at a time.
The steeples swam in amethyst,
The news like squirrels ran.

text:
The heaven below the heaven above
Obscured with ruddier hue.
Life's latitude leant over-full;
The judgment perished, too.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
The homeward bound and the outward bound,
The beautiful lost swimmer, the ennuye, the onanist, the female that
loves unrequited, the money-maker,
The actor and actress, those through with their parts and those
waiting to commence,
The affectionate boy, the husband and wife, the voter, the nominee
that is chosen and the nominee that has fail’d,
The great already known and the great any time after to-day,
The stammerer, the sick, the perfect-form’d, the homely,
The criminal that stood in the box, the judge that sat and sentenced
him, the fluent lawyers, the jury, the audience,
The laugher and weeper, the dancer, the midnight widow, the red squaw,
The consumptive, the erysipalite, the idiot, he that is wrong’d,
The antipodes, and every one between this and them in the dark,
I swear they are averaged now--one is no better than the other,
The night and sleep have liken’d them and restored them.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
The homeward-bound and the outward-bound,  
The lovely lost swimmer, the ennuye, the onanist, the maiden that  
loves unrequited, the money-maker,  
The actor and actress, those done with their parts and those  
waiting to begin,  
The loving boy, the husband and wife, the voter, the nominee  
that is chosen and the nominee that hath fail’d,  
The great already known and the great any time after to-day,  
The stammerer, the sick, the perfect-form’d, the homely,  
The criminal that stood in the dock, the judge that sat and sentenced  
him, the ready lawyers, the jury, the audience,  
The laugher and weeper, the dancer, the midnight widow, the red squaw,  
The consumptive, the erysipelatous, the idiot, he that is wrong’d,  
The antipodes, and every one between this and them in the dark,  
I swear they are even’d now—one is no better than the other,  
The night and sleep have made them like and restored them.
source_text:
The homeward bound and the outward bound,
The beautiful lost swimmer, the ennuye, the onanist, the female that
loves unrequited, the money-maker,
The actor and actress, those through with their parts and those
waiting to commence,
The affectionate boy, the husband and wife, the voter, the nominee
that is chosen and the nominee that has fail’d,
The great already known and the great any time after to-day,
The stammerer, the sick, the perfect-form’d, the homely,
The criminal that stood in the box, the judge that sat and sentenced
him, the fluent lawyers, the jury, the audience,
The laugher and weeper, the dancer, the midnight widow, the red squaw,
The consumptive, the erysipalite, the idiot, he that is wrong’d,
The antipodes, and every one between this and them in the dark,
I swear they are averaged now--one is no better than the other,
The night and sleep have liken’d them and restored them.

text:
Ye wild-eyed Muses, sing the Twins of Jove,
Whom the fair-ankled Leda, mixed in love
With mighty Saturn’s Heaven-obscuring Child,
On Taygetus, that lofty mountain wild,
Brought forth in joy: mild Pollux, void of blame,
And steed-subduing Castor, heirs of fame.
These are the Powers who earth-born mortals save
And ships, whose flight is swift along the wave.
When wintry tempests o’er the savage sea
Are raging, and the sailors tremblingly
Call on the Twins of Jove with prayer and vow,
Gathered in fear upon the lofty prow,
And sacrifice with snow-white lambs,—the wind
And the huge billow bursting close behind,
Even then beneath the weltering waters bear
The staggering ship—they suddenly appear,
On yellow wings rushing athwart the sky,
And lull the blasts in mute tranquillity,
And strew the waves on the white Ocean’s bed,
Fair omen of the voyage; from toil and dread
The sailors rest, rejoicing in the sight,
And plough the quiet sea in safe delight.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Ye wild-eyed Muses, sing the Twins of Jove,  
Whom fair-ankled Leda, blent in love  
With mighty Saturn’s heaven-obscuring Child,  
On Taygetus, that lofty mountain wild,  
Bore in gladness: gentle Pollux, free from blame,  
And steed-subduing Castor, heirs of fame.  
These are the Powers who earth-born mortals save  
And ships, whose swiftness skims along the wave.  
When wintry tempests o’er the savage sea  
Are roaring, and the sailors fearfully  
Invoke the Twins of Jove with prayer and vow,  
Assembled in affright upon the lofty prow,  
And sacrifice white lambs,—the wind, and then  
The huge wave bursting close behind again,  
Even then beneath the weltering waters bear  
The staggering ship—they suddenly appear,  
On yellow wings sweeping athwart the sky,  
And lull the blasts to voiceless tranquility,  
And strew the waves upon the white sea’s bed,  
Fair omen of the voyage; from toil and dread  
The sailors rest, rejoicing at the sight,  
And plough the quiet sea in safe delight.

Whom the fair-ankled Leda, mixed in love
With mighty Saturn’s Heaven-obscuring Child,
On Taygetus, that lofty mountain wild,
Brought forth in joy: mild Pollux, void of blame,
And steed-subduing Castor, heirs of fame.
These are the Powers who earth-born mortals save
And ships, whose flight is swift along the wave.
When wintry tempests o’er the savage sea
Are raging, and the sailors tremblingly
Call on the Twins of Jove with prayer and vow,
Gathered in fear upon the lofty prow,
And sacrifice with snow-white lambs,—the wind
And the huge billow bursting close behind,
Even then beneath the weltering waters bear
The staggering ship—they suddenly appear,
On yellow wings rushing athwart the sky,
And lull the blasts in mute tranquillity,
And strew the waves on the white Ocean’s bed,
Fair omen of the voyage; from toil and dread
The sailors rest, rejoicing in the sight,
And plough the quiet sea in safe delight.

text:
Of a’ the airts the wind can blaw,
I dearly like the west,
For there the bonie lassie lives,
The lassie I lo’e best:

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Of all the winds that sweep the sky,
I dearly prize the west,
For there the bonny lassie dwells,
The lassie I love best:

text:
CAMILLO:
Guards, lead him nearer the Lady Beatrice;
He shrinks from her regard like autumn’s leaf
From the keen breath of the serenest north.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
CAMILLO:
Guards, bring him nearer to the Lady Beatrice;
He cowers from her eye like the leaf of autumn
Before the sharp breath of the most tranquil north.
source_text:
CAMILLO:
Guards, lead him nearer the Lady Beatrice;
He shrinks from her regard like autumn’s leaf
From the keen breath of the serenest north.

text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone
Decidedly can try us;
He knows each chord, its various tone,
Each spring, its various bias:
Then at the balance let’s be mute,
We never can adjust it;
What’s done we partly may compute,
But know not what’s resisted.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone  
Can surely search and prove us;  
He knows each chord, its varied tone,  
Each spring, its several bias:  
Then at the balance let us cease,  
Nor seek to set it right;  
What’s done we may in part surmise,  
But know not what withstood it.
source_text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone
Decidedly can try us;
He knows each chord, its various tone,
Each spring, its various bias:
Then at the balance let’s be mute,
We never can adjust it;
What’s done we partly may compute,
But know not what’s resisted.

text:
By the mossy brink,
With me the Prince shall sit and think;
Shall muse in visioned Regency,
Rapt in bright dreams of dawning Royalty.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
By the moss-clad brink,
With me the Prince shall sit and think;
Shall muse in visioned Regency,
Enrapt in bright dreams of dawning Royalty.
source_text:
By the mossy brink,
With me the Prince shall sit and think;
Shall muse in visioned Regency,
Rapt in bright dreams of dawning Royalty.

text:
O, for a draught of vintage! that hath been
Cool'd a long age in the deep-delved earth,
Tasting of Flora and the country green,
Dance, and Provençal song, and sunburnt mirth!
O for a beaker full of the warm South,
Full of the true, the blushful Hippocrene,
With beaded bubbles winking at the brim,
And purple-stained mouth;
That I might drink, and leave the world unseen,
And with thee fade away into the forest dim:

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Strengthened in heart, yet sad, that aged man
I left, with interchange of looks and tears,
And lingering speech, and to the Camp began
My war. O’er many a mountain-chain which rears
Its hundred crests aloft, my spirit bears
My frame; o’er many a dale and many a moor,
And gaily now meseems serene earth wears
The blosmy spring’s star-bright investiture,
A vision which aught sad from sadness might allure.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Auld Neibour,
I’m three times doubly o’er your debtor,
For your auld-farrant, frien’ly letter;
Tho’ I maun say’t I doubt ye flatter,
Ye speak sae fair;
For my puir, silly, rhymin clatter
Some less maun sair.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Auld Neibour,
I’m thrice times doubly o’er your debtor,
For your auld-farrant, kindly letter;
Tho’ I maun say I doubt ye flatter,
Ye speak sae fair;
For my puir, silly, rhyming clatter
Some less maun fare.

text:
Hear me, ye venerable core,
As counsel for poor mortals
That frequent pass douce Wisdom’s door
For glaikit Folly’s portals:
I, for their thoughtless, careless sakes,
Would here propone defences—
Their donsie tricks, their black mistakes,
Their failings and mischances.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Hark to me, ye reverend core,
As counsel for poor mortals
Who oft do pass through Wisdom’s door
For Folly’s foolish portals:
I, for their heedless, careless sake,
Would here propose defences—
Their droll misdeeds, their dark mistakes,
Their failings and mischance.
source_text:

I, for their thoughtless, careless sakes,

Their donsie tricks, their black mistakes,
Their failings and mischances.

text:
Open mouth of my soul uttering gladness,
Eyes of my soul seeing perfection,
Natural life of me faithfully praising things,
Corroborating forever the triumph of things.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Open throat of my soul, pouring forth gladness,  
Eyes of my soul beholding perfection,  
Natural life within me, loyal in praise of things,  
Evermore attesting the triumph of things.
source_text:
Open mouth of my soul uttering gladness,
Eyes of my soul seeing perfection,
Natural life of me faithfully praising things,
Corroborating forever the triumph of things.

text:
Return again, fair Lesley,
Return to Caledonie!
That we may brag we hae a lass
There’s nane again sae bonie.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Come back once more, fair Lesley,
Come back to Caledonie!
That we may boast we hae a lass
There’s nane again sae bonie.
source_text:
Return again, fair Lesley,
Return to Caledonie!
That we may brag we hae a lass
There’s nane again sae bonie.

text:
And is this death?—The pyre has disappeared,
The Pestilence, the Tyrant, and the throng;
The flames grow silent—slowly there is heard
The music of a breath-suspending song,
Which, like the kiss of love when life is young,
Steeps the faint eyes in darkness sweet and deep;
With ever-changing notes it floats along,
Till on my passive soul there seemed to creep
A melody, like waves on wrinkled sands that leap.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
And is this death?—The pyre hath vanished clean,  
The Pestilence, the Tyrant, and the throng;  
The flames fall hush’d—by slow degrees is seen  
The music of a breath-detaining song,  
Which, like the kiss of love when life is young,  
Steeps the faint eyes in darkness sweet and deep;  
With ever-shifting notes it glides along,  
Till on my passive soul there seem’d to creep  
A melody, like waves on wrinkled sands that leap.
source_text:
And is this death?—The pyre has disappeared,
The Pestilence, the Tyrant, and the throng;
The flames grow silent—slowly there is heard
The music of a breath-suspending song,
Which, like the kiss of love when life is young,
Steeps the faint eyes in darkness sweet and deep;
With ever-changing notes it floats along,
Till on my passive soul there seemed to creep
A melody, like waves on wrinkled sands that leap.

text:
...
Seized with a sudden fancy for fresh meat,
He in his sacred crib deposited
The hollow lyre, and from the cavern sweet
Rushed with great leaps up to the mountain’s head,
Revolving in his mind some subtle feat
Of thievish craft, such as a swindler might
Devise in the lone season of dun night.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
...
Seized with a sudden longing for new meat,
He in his sacred crib laid down
The hollow lyre, and from the cavern sweet
Burst forth with mighty leaps to the mountain’s crown,
Turning in his mind some subtle feat
Of pilfering craft, such as a rogue might
Contrive in the solitary season of dark night.

text:
There is no stoppage and never can be stoppage,
If I, you, and the worlds, and all beneath or upon their surfaces,
were this moment reduced back to a pallid float, it would
not avail the long run,
We should surely bring up again where we now stand,
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
There is no halting, nor can there ever be halting,  
If I, thou, and the worlds, and all beneath or above their surfaces,  
were this very moment brought back to a pallid drift, it would  
not profit the long course,  
We should assuredly rise again where now we stand,  
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther.
source_text:

If I, you, and the worlds, and all beneath or upon their surfaces,
were this moment reduced back to a pallid float, it would
not avail the long run,
We should surely bring up again where we now stand,
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther.

text:
Others take finish, but the Republic is ever constructive and ever
keeps vista,
Others adorn the past, but you O days of the present, I adorn you,
O days of the future I believe in you--I isolate myself for your sake,
O America because you build for mankind I build for you,
O well-beloved stone-cutters, I lead them who plan with decision
and science,
Lead the present with friendly hand toward the future.
(Bravas to all impulses sending sane children to the next age!
But damn that which spends itself with no thought of the stain,
pains, dismay, feebleness, it is bequeathing.)

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Others attain fulfilment, but the Republic is still in the making and ever
holds the far horizon,
Others deck the past, but you O days of the present, I deck you,
O days of the future I trust in you--I withdraw apart for your sake,
O America because thou buildest for mankind I build for thee,
O well-beloved stone-cutters, I guide them who devise with resolve
and science,
Lead the present with a friendly hand toward the future.
(Bravas to all impulses sending sane children to the next age!
But damn that which spends itself with no thought of the stain,
pains, dismay, feebleness, it is bequeathing.)

text:
SEMICHORUS 2:
The world’s eyeless charioteer,
Destiny, is hurrying by!
What faith is crushed, what empire bleeds
Beneath her earthquake-footed steeds?
What eagle-winged victory sits
At her right hand? what shadow flits
Before? what splendour rolls behind?
Ruin and renovation cry
‘Who but We?’

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
But now the Lord’s ain trumpet touts,
Till a’ the hills are rairin,
And echoes back return the shouts;
Black Russell is na sparin:
His piercin words, like Highlan’ swords,
Divide the joints an’ marrow;
His talk o’ Hell, whare devils dwell,
Our vera “sauls does harrow”
Wi’ fright that day!

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
I see the blood wash’d entirely away from the axe,
Both blade and helve are clean,
They spirt no more the blood of European nobles, they clasp no more
the necks of queens.

text:
Amid the mountains, like a hunted beast,
He hid himself, and hunger, toil, and cold,
Month after month endured; it was a feast
Whene’er he found those globes of deep-red gold
Which in the woods the strawberry-tree doth bear,
Suspended in their emerald atmosphere.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
(O Mother--O Sisters dear!
If we are lost, no victor else has destroy’d us,
It is by ourselves we go down to eternal night.)

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Spirit of Nature! here
In this interminable wilderness
Of worlds, at whose involved immensity
Even soaring fancy staggers,
Here is thy fitting temple.
Yet not the lightest leaf
That quivers to the passing breeze
Is less instinct with thee,—
Yet not the meanest worm.
That lurks in graves and fattens on the dead,
Less shares thy eternal breath.
Spirit of Nature! thou
Imperishable as this glorious scene,
Here is thy fitting temple.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Spirit of Nature! here  
In this unending wilderness  
Of worlds, beneath whose folded vastness  
Even high-wing'd Fancy reels,  
Here stands thy meetest shrine.  
Yet not the lightest leaf  
That trembles at the passing gale  
Is less alive with thee,—  
Yet not the humblest worm  
That hides in graves and feeds upon the dead  
Less partakes thy eternal breath.  
Spirit of Nature! thou  
Imperishable as this glorious scene,  
Here stands thy meetest shrine.
source_text:

In this interminable wilderness
Of worlds, at whose involved immensity
Even soaring fancy staggers,
Here is thy fitting temple.
Yet not the lightest leaf
That quivers to the passing breeze
Is less instinct with thee,—
Yet not the meanest worm.
That lurks in graves and fattens on the dead,
Less shares thy eternal breath.
Spirit of Nature! thou
Imperishable as this glorious scene,
Here is thy fitting temple.

text:
‘’Twas like an eye which seemed to smile on me.
I watched, till by the sun made pale, it sank
Under the billows of the heaving sea;
But from its beams deep love my spirit drank,
And to my brain the boundless world now shrank
Into one thought—one image—yes, for ever!
Even like the dayspring, poured on vapours dank,
The beams of that one Star did shoot and quiver
Through my benighted mind—and were extinguished never.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
’Twas like an eye that seemed to smile on me.  
I gazed, until, by sunmade pallor waned, it sank  
Beneath the billows of the surging sea;  
Yet from its rays my spirit deep love drank,  
And to my brain the boundless world now shrank  
To one thought—one sole image—ay, for ever!  
Even as the dawn, on vapours dimly dank,  
The beams of that one Star did shoot and quiver  
Through my benighted mind—and were extinguished never.
source_text:
‘’Twas like an eye which seemed to smile on me.
I watched, till by the sun made pale, it sank
Under the billows of the heaving sea;
But from its beams deep love my spirit drank,
And to my brain the boundless world now shrank
Into one thought—one image—yes, for ever!
Even like the dayspring, poured on vapours dank,
The beams of that one Star did shoot and quiver
Through my benighted mind—and were extinguished never.

text:
I descend my western course, my sinews are flaccid,
Perfume and youth course through me and I am their wake.

text:
LUCRETIA [IN A HURRIED AND CONFUSED MANNER]:
I think he sleeps;
Yet, wake him not, I pray, spare me awhile,
He is a wicked and a wrathful man;
Should he be roused out of his sleep to-night,
Which is, I know, a hell of angry dreams,
It were not well; indeed it were not well.
Wait till day break...
[ASIDE.]
Oh, I am deadly sick!

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
LUCRETIA [IN A HURRIED AND CONFUSED MANNER]:
I think he sleeps;
Yet wake him not, I pray thee, grant me time awhile,
He is a wicked and a wrathful man;
Should he be roused from out his sleep to-night,
Which is, I know, a hell of angry dreams,
It were not good; indeed it were not good.
Wait till day break...
[ASIDE.]
Oh, I am deadly sick!

No shutter’d room or school can commune with me,
But roughs and little children better than they.

But rough men and small children better than they.

text:
‘For thou art mine, and I am thine,
‘Till the dreaded judgement day,
I am thine, and thou art mine—
Night is past—I must away.’

text:

Till that feared day of doom shall climb,
I am thine, and thou art mine—
Night is fled—I must depart.

text:
A further force of life
Developed from within, --
When Death lit all the shortness up,
And made the hurry plain.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Poet:
Fresh and rosy red the sun is mounting high,
On floats the sea in distant blue careering through its channels,
On floats the wind over the breast of the sea setting in toward land,
The great steady wind from west or west-by-south,
Floating so buoyant with milk-white foam on the waters.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Poet:
Fresh and rose-hued red the sun is climbing high,
There floats the sea in far-off blue, careering through its channels,
There floats the wind above the bosom of the sea, setting landward,
The great steadfast wind from west or west by south,
Floating so buoyant, with milk-white foam upon the waters.

text:
But I, grown shrewder, scan the skies
With a suspicious air, --
As children, swindled for the first,
All swindlers be, infer.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Sea of stretch’d ground-swells,
Sea breathing broad and convulsive breaths,
Sea of the brine of life and of unshovell’d yet always-ready graves,
Howler and scooper of storms, capricious and dainty sea,
I am integral with you, I too am of one phase and of all phases.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Sea of outspread ground-swell,  
Sea breathing wide, convulsive breaths,  
Sea of the brine of life and of graves unshoveled, yet ever ready,  
Howler and scooper of storms, capricious and dainty sea,  
I am one with thee, I too am of one phase and of all phases.

text:
With step as soft as wind it passed
O’er the heads of men—so fast
That they knew the presence there,
And looked,—but all was empty air.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
With tread as soft as wind it glided  
O’er the heads of men—so fleet  
That they felt the presence there,  
And looked,—but all was empty air.

text:
It’s hardly in a body’s pow’r
To keep, at times, frae being sour,
To see how things are shar’d;
How best o’ chiels are whiles in want,
While coofs on countless thousands rant,
And ken na how to wair’t;
But, Davie, lad, ne’er fash your head,
Tho’ we hae little gear;
We’re fit to win our daily bread,
As lang’s we’re hale and fier:
“Mair spier na, nor fear na,”
Auld age ne’er mind a feg;
The last o’t, the warst o’t
Is only but to beg.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
It’s scarce within a mortal’s pow’r  
At times to keep from turning sour,  
To mark how all things are dispensed;  
How best of men are often pinched,  
While fools on tens of thousands sprawl,  
And know not how to spend them all;  
But, Davie, lad, do not distress,  
Though little gear is ours to bless;  
We’re fit to win our daily bread,  
So long as we are stout and fed:  
“Ask no more, nor fear no more,”  
Old age ne’er heeds a fig;  
The worst of it, the end of it  
Is only just to beg.
source_text:

To keep, at times, frae being sour,
To see how things are shar’d;
How best o’ chiels are whiles in want,
While coofs on countless thousands rant,
And ken na how to wair’t;
But, Davie, lad, ne’er fash your head,
Tho’ we hae little gear;
We’re fit to win our daily bread,
As lang’s we’re hale and fier:
“Mair spier na, nor fear na,”
Auld age ne’er mind a feg;
The last o’t, the warst o’t
Is only but to beg.

text:
My tread scares the wood-drake and wood-duck on my distant and
day-long ramble,
They rise together, they slowly circle around.

text:
Fierce roars the midnight storm
O’er the wild mountain,
Dark clouds the night deform,
Swift rolls the fountain—

Over the savage mountain,  
Black clouds the night transform,  
Quickly rushes the fountain—

text:
I swear I see what is better than to tell the best,
It is always to leave the best untold.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Coffin that passes through lanes and streets,
Through day and night with the great cloud darkening the land,
With the pomp of the inloop’d flags with the cities draped in black,
With the show of the States themselves as of crape-veil’d women standing,
With processions long and winding and the flambeaus of the night,
With the countless torches lit, with the silent sea of faces and the
unbared heads,
With the waiting depot, the arriving coffin, and the sombre faces,
With dirges through the night, with the thousand voices rising strong
and solemn,
With all the mournful voices of the dirges pour’d around the coffin,
The dim-lit churches and the shuddering organs--where amid these
you journey,
With the tolling tolling bells’ perpetual clang,
Here, coffin that slowly passes,
I give you my sprig of lilac.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Coffin that fares through alleys and streets,  
Through day and through night with the great cloud darkening the land,  
With the pomp of the inloop’d flags, with the cities draped in black,  
With the show of the States themselves as of crape-veil’d women standing,  
With processions long and winding and the flambeaus of the night,  
With the countless torches kindled, with the silent sea of faces and the  
uncover’d heads,  
With the waiting depot, the arriving coffin, and the sombre faces,  
With dirges through the night, with the thousand voices mounting strong  
and solemn,  
With all the mournful voices of the dirges pour’d about the coffin,  
The dim-lit churches and the shuddering organs—where amid these  
thou journeyest,  
With the tolling, tolling bells’ perpetual clang,  
Here, coffin that slowest passest,  
I give thee my sprig of lilac.

With the waiting depot, the arriving coffin, and the sombre faces,

text:
ORSINO:
There is no need of that. Listen: you know
Olimpio, the castellan of Petrella
In old Colonna’s time; him whom your father
Degraded from his post? And Marzio,
That desperate wretch, whom he deprived last year
Of a reward of blood, well earned and due?

text:

Olimpio, the castellan of Petrella
In old Colonna’s day; the man your father
Cast down from his office? And Marzio,
That desperate wretch, whom he but last year
Denied the meed of blood, well earned and due?

text:
They say that 'time assuages,' --
Time never did assuage;
An actual suffering strengthens,
As sinews do, with age.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
They say that Time assuageth, --
Time never did assuage;
A very sorrow waxes strong,
As sinews do, with age.
source_text:

text:
It is not to diffuse you that you were born of your mother and
father, it is to identify you,
It is not that you should be undecided, but that you should be decided,
Something long preparing and formless is arrived and form’d in you,
You are henceforth secure, whatever comes or goes.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
It is not to dissolve thee that thou wert born of thy mother and
father, it is to know thee,
It is not that thou shouldst be wavering, but that thou shouldst be resolute,
Something long in making and shapeless is come and made in thee,
Thou art from hence secure, whatever cometh or departeth.
source_text:

father, it is to identify you,
It is not that you should be undecided, but that you should be decided,
Something long preparing and formless is arrived and form’d in you,
You are henceforth secure, whatever comes or goes.

text:
The quivering vapours of dim noontide,
Which like a sea o’er the warm earth glide,
In which every sound, and odour, and beam,
Move, as reeds in a single stream;

text:
Ashes denote that fire was;
Respect the grayest pile
For the departed creature's sake
That hovered there awhile.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Forms, qualities, lives, humanity, language, thoughts,
The ones known, and the ones unknown, the ones on the stars,
The stars themselves, some shaped, others unshaped,
Wonders as of those countries, the soil, trees, cities, inhabitants,
whatever they may be,
Splendid suns, the moons and rings, the countless combinations and effects,
Such-like, and as good as such-like, visible here or anywhere, stand
provided for a handful of space, which I extend my arm and
half enclose with my hand,
That containing the start of each and all, the virtue, the germs of all.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Forms, qualities, lives, humanity, speech, thoughts,  
The known ones, and the unknown, and those upon the stars,  
The stars themselves, some fashioned, others formless,  
Wonders like those countries, the soil, the trees, the cities, the dwellers,  
whatsoe'er they may be,  
Splendid suns, the moons and rings, the numberless mixtures and their workings,  
Such-like, and as fair as such-like, seen here or anywhere, lie  
set forth for a little space, which I stretch forth my arm and  
with my hand half compass,  
That holding the birth of each and all, the virtue, the seeds of all.

text:
I started early, took my dog,
And visited the sea;
The mermaids in the basement
Came out to look at me,

text:

And wandered down to sea;
The mermaids in the cellar-dim
Came forth to gaze on me,

text:
Now I will do nothing but listen,
To accrue what I hear into this song, to let sounds contribute toward it.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Now shall I do naught but hearken,  
To gather all I hear into this song, to let sounds lend their part thereto.
source_text:
Now I will do nothing but listen,
To accrue what I hear into this song, to let sounds contribute toward it.

text:
Far safer through an Abbey gallop,
The stones achase,
Than, moonless, one's own self encounter
In lonesome place.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Let others love the city,
And gaudy show, at sunny noon;
Gie me the lonely valley,
The dewy eve and rising moon,
Fair beaming, and streaming,
Her silver light the boughs amang;
While falling; recalling,
The amorous thrush concludes his sang;
There, dearest Chloris, wilt thou rove,
By wimpling burn and leafy shaw,
And hear my vows o’ truth and love,
And say, thou lo’es me best of a’.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Soft went the music the soft air along,
While fluent Greek a vowel'd undersong
Kept up among the guests, discoursing low
At first, for scarcely was the wine at flow;
But when the happy vintage touch'd their brains,
Louder they talk, and louder come the strains
Of powerful instruments:--the gorgeous dyes,
The space, the splendour of the draperies,
The roof of awful richness, nectarous cheer,
Beautiful slaves, and Lamia's self, appear,
Now, when the wine has done its rosy deed,
And every soul from human trammels freed,
No more so strange; for merry wine, sweet wine,
Will make Elysian shades not too fair, too divine.
Soon was God Bacchus at meridian height;
Flush'd were their cheeks, and bright eyes double bright:
Garlands of every green, and every scent
From vales deflower'd, or forest-trees branch-rent,
In baskets of bright osier'd gold were brought
High as the handles heap'd, to suit the thought
Of every guest; that each, as he did please,
Might fancy-fit his brows, silk-pillow'd at his ease.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Soft floated the music down the gentle air,
While fluent Greek, in vowelled undertone,
Was kept among the guests, discoursing low
At first, for scarcely yet had wine begun;
But when the fortunate vintage touch’d their brains,
The louder grew their speech, and louder rose the strains
Of potent instruments:--the gorgeous dyes,
The breadth, the splendour, and the draperies’ guise,
The roof of awful richness, nectarous cheer,
Fair slaves, and Lamia’s self, stood all forth near,
Now, when the wine hath done its rosy deed,
And every soul from human fetters freed,
No longer seem so strange; for merry wine, sweet wine,
Will make Elysian shades not over-fair, too divine.
Soon was God Bacchus at meridian height;
Flush’d were their cheeks, and bright eyes doubly bright:
Garlands of every green, and every scent
From vales despoil’d, or forest-branches rent,
In baskets of bright osier’d gold were brought
High as the handles heap’d, to suit the thought
Of every guest; that each, as he did please,
Might fancy-fit his brows, silk-pillow’d, at his ease.
source_text:
Soft went the music the soft air along,
While fluent Greek a vowel'd undersong
Kept up among the guests, discoursing low
At first, for scarcely was the wine at flow;
But when the happy vintage touch'd their brains,
Louder they talk, and louder come the strains
Of powerful instruments:--the gorgeous dyes,
The space, the splendour of the draperies,
The roof of awful richness, nectarous cheer,
Beautiful slaves, and Lamia's self, appear,
Now, when the wine has done its rosy deed,
And every soul from human trammels freed,
No more so strange; for merry wine, sweet wine,
Will make Elysian shades not too fair, too divine.
Soon was God Bacchus at meridian height;
Flush'd were their cheeks, and bright eyes double bright:
Garlands of every green, and every scent
From vales deflower'd, or forest-trees branch-rent,
In baskets of bright osier'd gold were brought
High as the handles heap'd, to suit the thought
Of every guest; that each, as he did please,
Might fancy-fit his brows, silk-pillow'd at his ease.

text:
Nor where it went, nor why it came
The second time to me,
Nor definitely what it was,
Have I the art to say.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Nor whither went, nor wherefore came  
The second time to me,  
Nor surely what it verily was,  
Have I the skill to say.
source_text:
Nor where it went, nor why it came
The second time to me,
Nor definitely what it was,
Have I the art to say.

text:
Even like a bark, which from a chasm of mountains,
Dark, vast and overhanging, on a river
Which there collects the strength of all its fountains,
Comes forth, whilst with the speed its frame doth quiver,
Sails, oars and stream, tending to one endeavour;
So, from that chasm of light a winged Form
On all the winds of heaven approaching ever
Floated, dilating as it came; the storm
Pursued it with fierce blasts, and lightnings swift and warm.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Even as a bark, which from a rift of mountains,  
Dark, huge, and beetling, on a river  
That there draws in the might of all its fountains,  
Comes forth, while with the speed its timbers quiver,  
Sails, oars, and stream, all bent on one endeavour;  
So, from that rift of light a winged Form  
On all the winds of heaven still drawing ever  
Floated, expanding as it drew: the storm  
Pursued it with fierce blasts, and lightnings swift and warm.
source_text:
Even like a bark, which from a chasm of mountains,
Dark, vast and overhanging, on a river
Which there collects the strength of all its fountains,
Comes forth, whilst with the speed its frame doth quiver,
Sails, oars and stream, tending to one endeavour;
So, from that chasm of light a winged Form
On all the winds of heaven approaching ever
Floated, dilating as it came; the storm
Pursued it with fierce blasts, and lightnings swift and warm.

text:
His bill an auger is,
His head, a cap and frill.
He laboreth at every tree, --
A worm his utmost goal.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
His bill an auger seems,  
His head, a cap and frill.  
He laboreth at every tree, --  
A worm his dearest goal.
source_text:

His head, a cap and frill.

text:
Her lips were parted, and the measured breath
Was now heard there;—her dark and intricate eyes
Orb within orb, deeper than sleep or death,
Absorbed the glories of the burning skies,
Which, mingling with her heart’s deep ecstasies,
Burst from her looks and gestures;—and a light
Of liquid tenderness, like love, did rise
From her whole frame, an atmosphere which quite
Arrayed her in its beams, tremulous and soft and bright.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Her lips were parted, and the gentle breath  
Was now discerned there;—her dark, entangled eyes  
Orb within orb, more deep than sleep or death,  
Drank in the glories of the burning skies,  
Which, blending with her heart’s profound ecstasies,  
Gushed from her looks and motions;—and a light  
Of fluid tenderness, like love, did rise  
From all her frame, an atmosphere that quite  
Arrayed her in its beams, trembling, soft, and bright.
source_text:
Her lips were parted, and the measured breath
Was now heard there;—her dark and intricate eyes
Orb within orb, deeper than sleep or death,
Absorbed the glories of the burning skies,
Which, mingling with her heart’s deep ecstasies,
Burst from her looks and gestures;—and a light
Of liquid tenderness, like love, did rise
From her whole frame, an atmosphere which quite
Arrayed her in its beams, tremulous and soft and bright.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Do the feasters gluttonous feast?
Do the corpulent sleepers sleep? have they lock’d and bolted doors?
Still be ours the diet hard, and the blanket on the ground,
Pioneers! O pioneers!

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Do the feasting gluttons feast?
Do the full-fed sleepers sleep? have they barred and bolted doors?
Still be ours the meagre fare, and the blanket on the ground,
Pioneers! O pioneers!
source_text:

Do the corpulent sleepers sleep? have they lock’d and bolted doors?
Still be ours the diet hard, and the blanket on the ground,
Pioneers! O pioneers!

text:
Of bronze and blaze
The north, to-night!
So adequate its forms,
So preconcerted with itself,
So distant to alarms, --
An unconcern so sovereign
To universe, or me,
It paints my simple spirit
With tints of majesty,
Till I take vaster attitudes,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance of them.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Of bronze and flame
The north, this night!
How meet its shapes,
How concerted with itself,
How far from any alarms, --
An unconcern so sovereign
Toward universe, or me,
It stains my simple spirit
With hues of majesty,
Till I assume more ample postures,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance in them.
source_text:
Of bronze and blaze
The north, to-night!
So adequate its forms,
So preconcerted with itself,
So distant to alarms, --
An unconcern so sovereign
To universe, or me,
It paints my simple spirit
With tints of majesty,
Till I take vaster attitudes,
And strut upon my stem,
Disdaining men and oxygen,
For arrogance of them.

text:
You dropped a purple ravelling in,
You dropped an amber thread;
And now you 've littered all the East
With duds of emerald!

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
You let a purple ravel fall,
You let an amber strand;
And now you’ve strewn the whole East
With scraps of emerald!
source_text:
You dropped a purple ravelling in,
You dropped an amber thread;
And now you 've littered all the East
With duds of emerald!

text:
The red-coat lads, wi’ black cockauds,
To meet them were na slaw, man;
They rush’d and push’d, and blude outgush’d
And mony a bouk did fa’, man:
The great Argyle led on his files,
I wat they glanced twenty miles;
They hough’d the clans like nine-pin kyles,
They hack’d and hash’d, while braid-swords, clash’d,
And thro’ they dash’d, and hew’d and smash’d,
Till fey men died awa, man.
La, la, la, la, &c.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
The red-coat lads, wi’ black cockauds,  
To meet them were na slack, man;  
They rush’d and push’d, and blude outgush’d,  
And mony a bouk fell back, man:  
The great Argyle led on his files,  
I wat they glanced full twenty miles;  
They hough’d the clans like nine-pin kyles,  
They hack’d and hash’d, while braid-swords clash’d,  
And thro’ they dash’d, and hew’d and smash’d,  
Till fey men died awa, man.  
La, la, la, la, &c.

text:
Friendship, mysterious cement of the soul!
Sweet’ner of Life, and solder of Society!
I owe thee much—Blair.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Friendship, hidden bond that binds the soul!
Sweetener of life, and solder of society!
Much do I owe thee—Blair.
source_text:
Friendship, mysterious cement of the soul!
Sweet’ner of Life, and solder of Society!
I owe thee much—Blair.

text:
They were two cousins, almost like to twins,
Except that from the catalogue of sins
Nature had rased their love—which could not be
But by dissevering their nativity.
And so they grew together like two flowers
Upon one stem, which the same beams and showers
Lull or awaken in their purple prime,
Which the same hand will gather—the same clime
Shake with decay. This fair day smiles to see
All those who love—and who e’er loved like thee,
Fiordispina? Scarcely Cosimo,
Within whose bosom and whose brain now glow
The ardours of a vision which obscure
The very idol of its portraiture.
He faints, dissolved into a sea of love;
But thou art as a planet sphered above;
But thou art Love itself—ruling the motion
Of his subjected spirit: such emotion
Must end in sin and sorrow, if sweet May
Had not brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
They were two cousins, near as twins to view,  
Save that from out the calendar of sins  
Nature had blotted their affection—torn  
Asunder only by their births forlorn.  
And so together they upgrew, like flowers  
That on one stem drink one sun, one rain of showers,  
That in their purple season either lull  
Or wake, the same hand gathering when full;  
The same fair clime shall wither them in death.  
This lovely day smiles upon all that breathe  
In love—and who loved ever as didst thou,  
Fiordispina? Hardly Cosimo, now  
Whose heart and brain are kindled through and through  
With such a vision as eclipses view  
Of that fair idol from whose form it came.  
He faints, dissolved in a great sea of flame;  
But thou art like a planet, throned above;  
But thou art very Love, that rules the move  
Of his enthralled soul: such passion must  
Find sin and sorrow, had not gentle August May  
Brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.
source_text:
They were two cousins, almost like to twins,
Except that from the catalogue of sins
Nature had rased their love—which could not be
But by dissevering their nativity.
And so they grew together like two flowers
Upon one stem, which the same beams and showers
Lull or awaken in their purple prime,
Which the same hand will gather—the same clime
Shake with decay. This fair day smiles to see
All those who love—and who e’er loved like thee,
Fiordispina? Scarcely Cosimo,
Within whose bosom and whose brain now glow
The ardours of a vision which obscure
The very idol of its portraiture.
He faints, dissolved into a sea of love;
But thou art as a planet sphered above;
But thou art Love itself—ruling the motion
Of his subjected spirit: such emotion
Must end in sin and sorrow, if sweet May
Had not brought forth this morn—your wedding-day.

text:
Year that trembled and reel’d beneath me!
Your summer wind was warm enough, yet the air I breathed froze me,
A thick gloom fell through the sunshine and darken’d me,
Must I change my triumphant songs? said I to myself,
Must I indeed learn to chant the cold dirges of the baffled?
And sullen hymns of defeat?

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Year that quiver’d and reel’d beneath me!
Your summer wind was warm enow, yet the air I drew did freeze me,
A heavy gloom fell through the sunshine and made darkness of me,
Must I change my conquering songs? said I within myself,
Must I indeed learn to intone the chill dirges of the baffled?
And moody hymns of overthrow?
source_text:
Year that trembled and reel’d beneath me!
Your summer wind was warm enough, yet the air I breathed froze me,
A thick gloom fell through the sunshine and darken’d me,
Must I change my triumphant songs? said I to myself,
Must I indeed learn to chant the cold dirges of the baffled?
And sullen hymns of defeat?

text:
And while I paus’d it came to me that what he really sang for was
not there only,
Nor for his mate nor himself only, nor all sent back by the echoes,
But subtle, clandestine, away beyond,
A charge transmitted and gift occult for those being born.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
And while I paused, it came to me that what he truly sang for was
not there alone,
Nor for his mate, nor for himself alone, nor for all the echoes thrown back,
But something delicate, secret, beyond and away,
A charge conveyed and hidden gift for those yet to be born.
source_text:

not there only,
Nor for his mate nor himself only, nor all sent back by the echoes,
But subtle, clandestine, away beyond,
A charge transmitted and gift occult for those being born.

text:
It was an influence which Keats, with the development of his own
character and genius, was rapidly outgrowing.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

nature and genius, was rapidly outgrowing.

text:
In this braw age o’ wit and lear,
Will nane the Shepherd’s whistle mair
Blaw sweetly in its native air,
And rural grace;
And, wi’ the far-fam’d Grecian, share
A rival place?

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
We may dream, in that long sleep,
That we are not those who weep;
E’en as Pleasure dreams of thee,
Life-deserting Misery,
Thou mayst dream of her with me.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
We may dream, in that prolonged sleep,
That we are not those that weep;
E’en as Pleasure dreams of thee,
Life-abandoning Misery,
Thou mayst dream of her with me.

text:
Now safe the stately sawmont sail,
And trouts bedropp’d wi’ crimson hail,
And eels, weel-ken’d for souple tail,
And geds for greed,
Since, dark in Death’s fish-creel, we wail
Tam Samson’s dead!

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Now safe the stately sawmont sail,
And trouts beprick’d wi’ crimson hail,
And eels, weel-ken’d for supple tail,
And geds for greed,
Since, dark in Death’s fish-creel, we bewail
Tam Samson’s dead!

text:
Is there a bard of rustic song,
Who, noteless, steals the crowds among,
That weekly this area throng,
O, pass not by!
But, with a frater-feeling strong,
Here, heave a sigh.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Is there a bard of homely song,
Who, unregarded, moves the throng,
That weekly to this place belong,
O, pass him by?
Nay, with a brother-feeling strong,
Here, breathe a sigh.

text:
Each and all like ministering angels were
For the Sensitive Plant sweet joy to bear,
Whilst the lagging hours of the day went by
Like windless clouds o’er a tender sky.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Each and all, like ministrant angels, were  
To the Sensitive Plant a sweet joy to bear,  
Whilst the lagging hours of day went by  
Like windless clouds across a tender sky.

text:
Little lamb, who made thee?
Does thou know who made thee,
Gave thee life, and bid thee feed
By the stream and o’er the mead;
Gave thee clothing of delight,
Softest clothing, woolly, bright;
Gave thee such a tender voice,
Making all the vales rejoice?
Little lamb, who made thee?
Does thou know who made thee?

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
Who built this little Alban house
And shut the windows down so close
My spirit cannot see?
Who 'll let me out some gala day,
With implements to fly away,
Passing pomposity?

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Who reared this little Alban house  
And drew the windows down so close  
My spirit may not see?  
Who’ll let me out some gala day,  
With engines fit to bear me away,  
Beyond all pomp and show?
source_text:
Who built this little Alban house
And shut the windows down so close
My spirit cannot see?
Who 'll let me out some gala day,
With implements to fly away,
Passing pomposity?

text:
I am he who walks the States with a barb’d tongue, questioning every
one I meet,
Who are you that wanted only to be told what you knew before?
Who are you that wanted only a book to join you in your nonsense?

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
I am he who roams the States with a barbéd tongue, and questions each
one I encounter,
Who art thou that didst desire only to be told what thou knewest ere?
Who art thou that didst desire only a book to keep thee company in thy folly?
source_text:

one I meet,

Who are you that wanted only a book to join you in your nonsense?

text:
IONE:
Ah me! sweet sister,
The stream of sound has ebbed away from us,
And you pretend to rise out of its wave,
Because your words fall like the clear, soft dew
Shaken from a bathing wood-nymph’s limbs and hair.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
IONE:  
Ah, me! dear sister,  
The tide of sound hath ebbed away from us,  
And thou dost feign to rise from out its wave,  
Because thy words fall like the lucid, tender dew  
Shaken from a bathing wood-nymph’s limbs and hair.
source_text:
IONE:
Ah me! sweet sister,
The stream of sound has ebbed away from us,
And you pretend to rise out of its wave,
Because your words fall like the clear, soft dew
Shaken from a bathing wood-nymph’s limbs and hair.

text:
Leeze me on drink! it gies us mair
Than either school or college;
It kindles wit, it waukens lear,
It pangs us fou o’ knowledge:
Be’t whisky-gill or penny wheep,
Or ony stronger potion,
It never fails, or drinkin deep,
To kittle up our notion,
By night or day.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Praise be to drink! it brings us mair
Than either school or college;
It kindles wit, it wakens lear,
It fills us fou wi’ knowledge:
Be’t whisky-gill or penny wheep,
Or ony sterner potion,
It never fails, when drink we deep,
To quicken up our notion,
By night or day.

text:
It is well--against such I say not a word, I am their poet also,
But behold! such swiftly subside, burnt up for religion’s sake,
For not all matter is fuel to heat, impalpable flame, the essential
life of the earth,
Any more than such are to religion.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
It is well—against such I speak not a word, I am their poet also,
Yet lo! such quickly fade, consumed for religion’s sake,
For not all matter is fuel for heat, the impalpable flame, the essential
life of the earth,
Even as such are not for religion.

text:
An’ now we’re dern’d in dens and hollows,
And hunted, as was William Wallace,
Wi’ constables-thae blackguard fallows,
An’ sodgers baith;
But Gude preserve us frae the gallows,
That shamefu’ death!

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
An’ noo we’re pent in dens an’ hollows,
And chased, as was brave William Wallace,
Wi’ constables—thae blackguard fallows,
An’ sodgers baith;
But Gude preserve us frae the gallows,
That shamefu’ death!
source_text:

An’ sodgers baith;
But Gude preserve us frae the gallows,
That shamefu’ death!

text:
But there is ane aboon the lave,
Has wit, and sense, an’ a’ that;
A bonie lass, I like her best,
And wha a crime dare ca’ that?
For a’ that, &c.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Yet there is one above the lave,
That hath some wit, and sense, and that;
A bonny lass, I like her best,
And who would dare call that a sin?
For a’ that, &c.
source_text:

Has wit, and sense, an’ a’ that;

text:
He drove them wandering o’er the sandy way,
But, being ever mindful of his craft,
Backward and forward drove he them astray,
So that the tracks which seemed before, were aft;
His sandals then he threw to the ocean spray,
And for each foot he wrought a kind of raft
Of tamarisk, and tamarisk-like sprigs,
And bound them in a lump with withy twigs.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
He led them ranging o’er the sandy way,  
Yet, ever mindful of his cunning art,  
Backward and forward still he made them stray,  
Till tracks that seemed before were found apart;  
Then to the ocean spray he cast his shoe,  
And for each foot he fashioned, stout and light,  
A kind of raft of tamarisk and boughs,  
And bound them fast in one with withy twigs.
source_text:

But, being ever mindful of his craft,
Backward and forward drove he them astray,
So that the tracks which seemed before, were aft;
His sandals then he threw to the ocean spray,
And for each foot he wrought a kind of raft
Of tamarisk, and tamarisk-like sprigs,
And bound them in a lump with withy twigs.

text:
Poor Man, the flie, aft bizzes by,
And aft, as chance he comes thee nigh,
Thy damn’d auld elbow yeuks wi’joy
And hellish pleasure!
Already in thy fancy’s eye,
Thy sicker treasure.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Poor Man, the fly, doth oft hum past thy side,  
And oft, by chance, as he draweth thee nigh,  
Thy cursed old elbow itcheth up for joy  
And hellish pleasure!  
Already in thy fancy’s eye,  
Thy sicker treasure.

And hellish pleasure!
Already in thy fancy’s eye,
Thy sicker treasure.

text:
'T is an instant's play,
'T is a fond ambush,
Just to make bliss
Earn her own surprise!

text:
I sat beside my warpin-wheel,
And aye I ca’d it roun’;
But every shot and evey knock,
My heart it gae a stoun.
To the weaver’s, &c.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
I sat me by my warpin-wheel,
And aye I drave it round;
But every shot, and every knock,
My heart it gave a wound.
To the weaver’s, &c.

text:
That fire is scorching! Oh! I came,
From the caverned depth of Hell,
My fleeting false Rodolph to claim,
Mighty one! I know thee well.—

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Swiftly I shrivel at the thought of God,
At Nature and its wonders, Time and Space and Death,
But that I, turning, call to thee O soul, thou actual Me,
And lo, thou gently masterest the orbs,
Thou matest Time, smilest content at Death,
And fillest, swellest full the vastnesses of Space.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Divine am I inside and out, and I make holy whatever I touch or am
touch’d from,
The scent of these arm-pits aroma finer than prayer,
This head more than churches, bibles, and all the creeds.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Divine am I within and without, and I hallow all I lay my hand on, or am  
touch’d by,  
The fragrance of these arm-pits, an aroma finer than prayer,  
This head more than churches, bibles, and all the creeds.

text:
O, bitter mockery of the pompous bier,
While down the wretched Vital Part is driven!
The cave-lodged Beggar, with a conscience clear,
Expires in rags, unknown, and goes to Heaven.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
O, bitter mockery of the gaudy bier,
While downward is the hapless Vital driven!
The cave-enshrined Beggar, with a conscience clear,
In rags expires, unnoted, and to Heaven.

text:
THIRD VOICE (FROM THE AIR):
I had clothed, since Earth uprose,
Its wastes in colours not their own,
And oft had my serene repose
Been cloven by many a rending groan.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
THIRD VOICE (FROM THE AIR):
Since Earth uprose, I had arrayed
Its wastes in hues not their own,
And oft my serene rest had been stayed
By many a rending groan.
source_text:

text:
And so, when all the time had failed,
Without external sound,
Each bound the other's crucifix,
We gave no other bond.

In raptures sweet, this hour we meet,
Wi’ mutual love an’ a’ that;
But for how lang the flie may stang,
Let inclination law that.
For a’ that, &c.

text:
As one by one withdraw the lofty actors,
From that great play on history’s stage eterne,
That lurid, partial act of war and peace--of old and new contending,
Fought out through wrath, fears, dark dismays, and many a long suspense;
All past--and since, in countless graves receding, mellowing,
Victor’s and vanquish’d--Lincoln’s and Lee’s--now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days--and equal to the days!
Thou from the prairies!--tangled and many-vein’d and hard has been thy part,
To admiration has it been enacted!

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
As one by one the stately players vanish,
From that vast drama on history’s endless stage,
That glaring, partisan scene of war and peace—of old and new in conflict,
Wrought through rage, through fear, through midnight dread, and many a weary pause;
All gone—and since, in numberless graves receding, softening,
Conqueror’s and vanquish’d—Lincoln’s and Lee’s—now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days—and match’d to the days!
Thou from the prairies!—tangled and many-vein’d and hard hath been thy lot,
To wonder has it been performed!
source_text:
As one by one withdraw the lofty actors,
From that great play on history’s stage eterne,
That lurid, partial act of war and peace--of old and new contending,
Fought out through wrath, fears, dark dismays, and many a long suspense;
All past--and since, in countless graves receding, mellowing,
Victor’s and vanquish’d--Lincoln’s and Lee’s--now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days--and equal to the days!
Thou from the prairies!--tangled and many-vein’d and hard has been thy part,
To admiration has it been enacted!

text:
While simple-hearted neighbors
Chat of the 'early dead,'
We, prone to periphrasis,
Remark that birds have fled!

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:

Converse of the "early dead,"
We, prone to circumlocution,
Observe that birds are fled!
source_text:

text:
Their moss rotted off them, flake by flake,
Till the thick stalk stuck like a murderer’s stake,
Where rags of loose flesh yet tremble on high,
Infecting the winds that wander by.

text:
Their moss had rotted from them, flake by flake,
Till the stout stalk stood like a murderer’s stake,
Where shreds of loosened flesh still quiver aloft,
And taint the winds that drift along and waft.

Till the thick stalk stuck like a murderer’s stake,
Where rags of loose flesh yet tremble on high,

text:

Nor heedless by the way
Hint that within the riddle
One will walk to-day!

text:
(Still here I carry my old delicious burdens,
I carry them, men and women, I carry them with me wherever I go,
I swear it is impossible for me to get rid of them,
I am fill’d with them, and I will fill them in return.)

text:

I bear them, men and women, I bear them with me whereso I wander,
I swear it is not possible for me to be rid of them,
I am fill’d with them, and I shall fill them again in return.)

text:
The hoary Sire—the mortal stroke,
Long, long be pleas’d to spare;
To bless this little filial flock,
And show what good men are.

text:

To bless this little filial flock,  
And show what good men are.

text:
Ma femme! for the brood beyond us and of us,
For those who belong here and those to come,
I exultant to be ready for them will now shake out carols stronger
and haughtier than have ever yet been heard upon earth.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Ma femme! for the brood beyond us and of us,
For those who belong here and those to come,
I, exultant, to be ready for them, will now shake forth carols stronger
and loftier than have yet been heard upon earth.

text:
Curst Common-sense, that imp o’ hell,
Cam in wi’ Maggie Lauder;
But Oliphant aft made her yell,
An’ Russell sair misca’d her:
This day Mackinlay taks the flail,
An’ he’s the boy will blaud her!
He’ll clap a shangan on her tail,
An’ set the bairns to daud her
Wi’ dirt this day.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Curst Common-sense, that imp o’ hell,
Came in wi’ Maggie Lauder;
But Oliphant aft made her yell,
An’ Russell sair misca’d her:
This day Mackinlay grips the flail,
An’ he’s the lad that’ll blaud her!
He’ll clap a shangan on her tail,
An’ set the bairns to daud her
Wi’ dirt this day.
source_text:
Curst Common-sense, that imp o’ hell,
Cam in wi’ Maggie Lauder;
But Oliphant aft made her yell,
An’ Russell sair misca’d her:
This day Mackinlay taks the flail,
An’ he’s the boy will blaud her!

text:
And who art thou sad shade?
Gigantic, visionary, thyself a visionary,
With majestic limbs and pious beaming eyes,
Spreading around with every look of thine a golden world,
Enhuing it with gorgeous hues.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
And who art thou, melancholy shade?  
Gigantic, visionary, thyself a vision,  
With sovereign limbs and pious, shining eyes,  
Around thee, with each glance, a golden world thou spreadst,  
Investing it with glorious hues.
source_text:
And who art thou sad shade?
Gigantic, visionary, thyself a visionary,
With majestic limbs and pious beaming eyes,
Spreading around with every look of thine a golden world,
Enhuing it with gorgeous hues.

text:
I give nothing as duties,
What others give as duties I give as living impulses,
(Shall I give the heart’s action as a duty?)

text:

What others yield as duties I yield as living impulses,
(Shall I make the heart’s action a duty?)

text:
The men cast out in party-matches,
Then sowther a’ in deep debauches.
Ae night they’re mad wi’ drink an’ whoring,
Niest day their life is past enduring.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
The men, flung out in party-factions,
Then settle a’ in dark debauches.
Ae night they’re daft wi’ drink and whoring,
The neist day life is past enduring.

text:
The sun was sinking in the west,
The birds sang sweet in ilka grove;
His cheek to hers he fondly laid,
And whisper’d thus his tale o’ love:

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
I would have added—‘is all here amiss?—’
But a voice answered—‘Life!’—I turned, and knew
(O Heaven, have mercy on such wretchedness!)

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
I would have said—‘Is all here out of tune?’—
But then a voice replied—‘Life!’—I turned, and knew
(O Heaven, show mercy unto such wretchedness!)
source_text:

text:
Youth, large, lusty, loving--youth full of grace, force, fascination,
Do you know that Old Age may come after you with equal grace,
force, fascination?

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Youth, ample, ardent, amorous—youth brimful of grace, of vigor, of enchantment,  
Know you that Age may follow hard upon you with equal grace,  
force, enchantment?
source_text:

Do you know that Old Age may come after you with equal grace,
force, fascination?

"Their bed-time 't is," she said;

When April woods are red."

text:

“Their bed-time now,” she said;
“The bumble-bees shall wake them
When April woods burn red.”

text:
I had no time to hate, because
The grave would hinder me,
And life was not so ample I
Could finish enmity.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
I had no time to hate, because  
The grave would stay my hand,  
And life was not so spacious I  
Could bring my enmity to end.

The grave would hinder me,

text:
And old Silenus, shaking a green stick
Of lilies, and the wood-gods in a crew
Came, blithe, as in the olive copses thick
Cicadae are, drunk with the noonday dew:
And Dryope and Faunus followed quick,
Teasing the God to sing them something new;
Till in this cave they found the lady lone,
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
And aged Silenus, wavering a green staff  
Of lilies, and the wood-gods in a band  
Came, merry, as in the olive thickets thick  
The cicadae come, with noonday dew made drunk:  
And Dryope and Faunus came apace,  
Baiting the God to sing them somewhat new;  
Till in this cave they found the lady lone,  
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.
source_text:
And old Silenus, shaking a green stick
Of lilies, and the wood-gods in a crew
Came, blithe, as in the olive copses thick
Cicadae are, drunk with the noonday dew:
And Dryope and Faunus followed quick,
Teasing the God to sing them something new;
Till in this cave they found the lady lone,
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.

text:
Without shame the man I like knows and avows the deliciousness of his sex,
Without shame the woman I like knows and avows hers.

Without a blush the woman I like knows and avows hers.

text:
But, dreary tho’ the moments fleet,
O let me think we yet shall meet;
That only ray of solace sweet,
Can on thy Chloris shine, Love!
O wert thou, &c.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Yet, though the moments mournful flee,
O let me deem we yet shall be;
That single beam of solace free
May still on thy Chloris fall, Love!
O wert thou, &c.

text:
Follow to the deep wood’s weeds,
Follow to the wild-briar dingle,
Where we seek to intermingle,
And the violet tells her tale
To the odour-scented gale,
For they two have enough to do
Of such work as I and you.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Follow unto the deep wood’s weeds,
Follow unto the wild-briar dingle,
Where we seek to blend and mingle,
And the violet breathes her tale
To the odour-laden gale,
For those twain have enough to do
Of such task as I and you.
source_text:
Follow to the deep wood’s weeds,
Follow to the wild-briar dingle,
Where we seek to intermingle,
And the violet tells her tale
To the odour-scented gale,
For they two have enough to do
Of such work as I and you.

text:
And so his Soul would not be gay,
But moaned within him; like a fawn
Moaning within a cave, it lay
Wounded and wasting, day by day,
Till all its life of life was gone.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
And so his Soul would not be glad,  
But sighed within him; like a doe  
That sighs within a cavern sad,  
It lay maimed and waning, day by day,  
Till all its vital life was gone.
source_text:
And so his Soul would not be gay,
But moaned within him; like a fawn
Moaning within a cave, it lay
Wounded and wasting, day by day,
Till all its life of life was gone.

The maddest, quickest by;
But Nature lost the date of this,
And left it in the sky.

text:
One’s-self I sing, a simple separate person,
Yet utter the word Democratic, the word En-Masse.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
As Highland craigs by thunder cleft,
When lightnings fire the stormy lift,
Hurl down with crashing rattle;
As flames among a hundred woods,
As headlong foam from a hundred floods,
Such is the rage of Battle.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
As Highland cliffs by thunder riven,
When lightnings kindle the stormy heaven,
Are dashed with crashing rattle;
As flames amid a hundred woods,
As headlong foam from hundred floods,
Such is the rage of Battle.
