text:
O the magnet! the flesh over and over!
Go, dear friend, if need be give up all else, and commence to-day to
inure yourself to pluck, reality, self-esteem, definiteness,
elevatedness,
Rest not till you rivet and publish yourself of your own Personality.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
O the magnet! the flesh again and again!
Go, dear friend, if need be give up all else, and begin to-day to
school yourself to pluck, reality, self-respect, exactness,
loftiness,
Rest not till you rivet and proclaim yourself out of your own Personality.
source_text:
O the magnet! the flesh over and over!
Go, dear friend, if need be give up all else, and commence to-day to
inure yourself to pluck, reality, self-esteem, definiteness,
elevatedness,
Rest not till you rivet and publish yourself of your own Personality.

text:
Eagle! why soarest thou above that tomb?
To what sublime and star-ypaven home
Floatest thou?—
I am the image of swift Plato’s spirit,
Ascending heaven; Athens doth inherit
His corpse below.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Eagle! why dost thou mount above that tomb?
To what high and star-pavèd heaven-home
Dost thou sail?—
I am the likeness of fleet Plato’s soul,
Ascending unto heaven; Athens holds
His body here below.
source_text:
Eagle! why soarest thou above that tomb?
To what sublime and star-ypaven home
Floatest thou?—
I am the image of swift Plato’s spirit,
Ascending heaven; Athens doth inherit
His corpse below.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
‘Time passed, I know not whether months or years;
For day, nor night, nor change of seasons made
Its note, but thoughts and unavailing tears:
And I became at last even as a shade,
A smoke, a cloud on which the winds have preyed,
Till it be thin as air; until, one even,
A Nautilus upon the fountain played,
Spreading his azure sail where breath of Heaven
Descended not, among the waves and whirlpools driven.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
‘Time passed—I know not whether months or years;
For day, nor night, nor turn of seasons made
Its mark, but thoughts and tears that nothing cheers:
And I grew, in the end, as one half-shade,
A smoke, a cloud the winds have worn and frayed,
Till it was thin as air; until, one eve,
A Nautilus upon the fountain strayed,
Unfurling his blue sail where Heaven’s breath did not leave,
Among the waves and whirling eddies driven.
source_text:
‘Time passed, I know not whether months or years;
For day, nor night, nor change of seasons made
Its note, but thoughts and unavailing tears:
And I became at last even as a shade,
A smoke, a cloud on which the winds have preyed,
Till it be thin as air; until, one even,
A Nautilus upon the fountain played,
Spreading his azure sail where breath of Heaven
Descended not, among the waves and whirlpools driven.

text:
It’s no the frosty winter wind,
It’s no the driving drift and snaw;
But aye the tear comes in my e’e,
To think on him that’s far awa.

text:

But aye the tear wells in my e’e,
To think on him that’s far awa.
source_text:

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
And how to the Immortals every one
A portion was assigned of all that is;
But chief Mnemosyne did Maia’s son
Clothe in the light of his loud melodies;—
And, as each God was born or had begun,
He in their order due and fit degrees
Sung of his birth and being—and did move
Apollo to unutterable love.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
And unto all the Immortals, every one,
A share was dealt of all that doth subsist;
But chief Mnemosyne did Maia’s son
Array in light of his resounding hymns;—
And, as each God was born, or first begun,
In due and ordered ranks, and fitting lists,
He sang their birth and being—and did move
Apollo unto love beyond all tongue.
source_text:
And how to the Immortals every one
A portion was assigned of all that is;
But chief Mnemosyne did Maia’s son
Clothe in the light of his loud melodies;—
And, as each God was born or had begun,
He in their order due and fit degrees
Sung of his birth and being—and did move
Apollo to unutterable love.

text:
As children caper when they wake,
Merry that it is morn,
My flowers from a hundred cribs
Will peep, and prance again.

text:
l. 288. Like . . . among. Take this line word by word, and see how
many different ideas combine to make the eerily unmatched effect.
source_text:

text:
Beat! beat! drums!--blow! bugles! blow!
Over the traffic of cities--over the rumble of wheels in the streets;
Are beds prepared for sleepers at night in the houses? no sleepers
must sleep in those beds,
No bargainers’ bargains by day--no brokers or speculators--would
they continue?
Would the talkers be talking? would the singer attempt to sing?
Would the lawyer rise in the court to state his case before the judge?
Then rattle quicker, heavier drums--you bugles wilder blow.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Throb! throb, ye drums!--blare! bugles, blare!
Above the traffic of cities--above the clamor of wheels in the streets;
Are couches prepared for sleepers by night in the houses? no sleepers
may lie in those couches,
No trader’s bargains by day--no brokers or speculators--should
they endure?
Would the talkers be talking? would the singer essay to sing?
Would the lawyer rise in the court to plead his cause before the judge?
Then clatter swifter, weightier drums--ye bugles, wilder, blow.
source_text:
Beat! beat! drums!--blow! bugles! blow!
Over the traffic of cities--over the rumble of wheels in the streets;
Are beds prepared for sleepers at night in the houses? no sleepers
must sleep in those beds,
No bargainers’ bargains by day--no brokers or speculators--would
they continue?
Would the talkers be talking? would the singer attempt to sing?
Would the lawyer rise in the court to state his case before the judge?
Then rattle quicker, heavier drums--you bugles wilder blow.

text:
I behold the mariners of the world,
Some are in storms, some in the night with the watch on the lookout,
Some drifting helplessly, some with contagious diseases.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:

Some are in tempests, some in the night with the watch set on the lookout,  
Some drifting helplessly, some stricken with contagious diseases.

text:
Word over all, beautiful as the sky,
Beautiful that war and all its deeds of carnage must in time be
utterly lost,
That the hands of the sisters Death and Night incessantly softly
wash again, and ever again, this solid world;
For my enemy is dead, a man divine as myself is dead,
I look where he lies white-faced and still in the coffin--I draw near,
Bend down and touch lightly with my lips the white face in the coffin.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Word above all, as lovely as the heavens,
Lovely that war and all its works of slaughter must in time be
utterly forgotten,
That the hands of the sisters Death and Night, without cease, with gentle
soothing, wash again, and yet again, this steadfast world;
For my foe is dead, a man as godlike as myself is dead,
I gaze where he lies pallid and still in the coffin—I draw nigh,
I bend and lightly touch with my lips the white face in the coffin.
source_text:

For my enemy is dead, a man divine as myself is dead,
I look where he lies white-faced and still in the coffin--I draw near,
Bend down and touch lightly with my lips the white face in the coffin.

text:
The two old, simple problems ever intertwined,
Close home, elusive, present, baffled, grappled.
By each successive age insoluble, pass’d on,
To ours to-day--and we pass on the same.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:

By each succeeding age unmastered, pass’d along,  
To ours at last—and we pass on the selfsame.

text:
Is this then a touch? quivering me to a new identity,
Flames and ether making a rush for my veins,
Treacherous tip of me reaching and crowding to help them,
My flesh and blood playing out lightning to strike what is hardly
different from myself,
On all sides prurient provokers stiffening my limbs,
Straining the udder of my heart for its withheld drip,
Behaving licentious toward me, taking no denial,
Depriving me of my best as for a purpose,
Unbuttoning my clothes, holding me by the bare waist,
Deluding my confusion with the calm of the sunlight and pasture-fields,
Immodestly sliding the fellow-senses away,
They bribed to swap off with touch and go and graze at the edges of me,
No consideration, no regard for my draining strength or my anger,
Fetching the rest of the herd around to enjoy them a while,
Then all uniting to stand on a headland and worry me.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Is this then a touch? quivering me into a newer self,
Flame and ether hurrying up through my veins,
False and traitorous end of me reaching and thronging to aid them,
My flesh and blood casting out lightning to smite what is scarcely
other than myself,
On every side lewd tempters hardening my limbs,
Milking the udder of my heart for its withheld drop,
Using me wantonly, granting no refusal,
Robbing me of my choicest as though for some design,
Unbuttoning my garments, gripping me by the naked waist,
Beguiling my bewilderment with the quiet of sunlight and fieldward pastures,
Shamelessly slipping the fellow-senses aside,
They were bribed to exchange themselves for touch and to crop at my borders,
No pity, no heed for my ebbing strength or my wrath,
Calling the rest of the herd to come and feast on them awhile,
Then all together to stand on a headland and harry me.

No consideration, no regard for my draining strength or my anger,

text:
No bone had he to bind him,
His speech was like the push
Of numerous humming-birds at once
From a superior bush.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
No bone had he to hold him,
His utterance was the gush
Of countless humming-birds at once
From an upper bush.
source_text:
No bone had he to bind him,
His speech was like the push

text:
And you, farewell! whose merits claim
Justly that highest badge to wear:
Heav’n bless your honour’d noble name,
To Masonry and Scotia dear!
A last request permit me here,—
When yearly ye assemble a’,
One round, I ask it with a tear,
To him, the Bard that’s far awa.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Swift to thy spell a shuddering hum like distant thunder rolls,
Lo, where the arm’d men hasten--lo, mid the clouds of dust the glint
of bayonets,
I see the grime-faced cannoneers, I mark the rosy flash amid the
smoke, I hear the cracking of the guns;
Nor war alone--thy fearful music-song, wild player, brings every
sight of fear,
The deeds of ruthless brigands, rapine, murder--I hear the cries for help!
I see ships foundering at sea, I behold on deck and below deck the
terrible tableaus.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Swift at thy charm a shivering murmur like far thunder rolls,
Lo, where the armored men make haste—lo, through the dust-clouds the
gleam
of bayonets,
I see the soot-stained cannoneers, I note the crimson flash amid the
smoke, I hear the gun-roar cracking;
Nor war alone—thy dread music-song, wild player, summons every
sight of fear,
The deeds of merciless brigands, rapine, murder—I hear the cries for aid!
I see ships founder upon the sea, I behold on deck and under deck the
awful tableaux.
source_text:

I see the grime-faced cannoneers, I mark the rosy flash amid the
smoke, I hear the cracking of the guns;
Nor war alone--thy fearful music-song, wild player, brings every
sight of fear,
The deeds of ruthless brigands, rapine, murder--I hear the cries for help!
I see ships foundering at sea, I behold on deck and below deck the
terrible tableaus.

text:
O resistless restless race!
O beloved race in all! O my breast aches with tender love for all!
O I mourn and yet exult, I am rapt with love for all,
Pioneers! O pioneers!

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
O unstoppable, wandering race!
O cherished race in all! O my breast aches with gentle love for all!
O I grieve and yet rejoice, I am carried away with love for all,
Pioneers! O pioneers!

text:
The bee is not afraid of me,
I know the butterfly;
The pretty people in the woods
Receive me cordially.

The comely people of the wood  
Receive me cordially.

text:
I hear and behold God in every object, yet understand God not in the least,
Nor do I understand who there can be more wonderful than myself.

text:
dismal cirque
Of Druid stones, upon a forlorn moor,
When the chill rain begins at shut of eve,
In dull November, and their chancel vault,
The heaven itself, is blinded throughout night.

Thou, mother of my mortal part,
With cruelty didst mould my heart,
And with false self-deceiving tears
Didst blind my nostrils, eyes, and ears,

text:
And thus, without a wing,
Or service of a keel,
Our summer made her light escape
Into the beautiful.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Poet:
O A new song, a free song,
Flapping, flapping, flapping, flapping, by sounds, by voices clearer,
By the wind’s voice and that of the drum,
By the banner’s voice and child’s voice and sea’s voice and father’s voice,
Low on the ground and high in the air,
On the ground where father and child stand,
In the upward air where their eyes turn,
Where the banner at daybreak is flapping.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
CHORUS OF THE THREE:
The Angels draw strength from Thy glance,
Though no one comprehend Thee may;—
Thy world’s unwithered countenance
Is bright as on Creation’s day.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text: Yes, it had come to “Death to the Republic!” everywhere—no voice of dissent was heard. All England was marching against us! Truly, this was more than I had bargained for.
source_text: Yes, it was now “Death to the Republic!” everywhere--not a dissenting voice. All England was marching against us! Truly, this was more than I had bargained for.

text:
Piteous she look'd on dead and senseless things,
Asking for her lost Basil amorously;
And with melodious chuckle in the strings
Of her lorn voice, she oftentimes would cry
After the Pilgrim in his wanderings,
To ask him where her Basil was; and why
'Twas hid from her: "For cruel 'tis," said she,
"To steal my Basil-pot away from me."

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
With piteous gaze she on dead, senseless things  
Would look, her lost Basil amorously seeking;  
And, with a tuneful quaver in the strings  
Of her forlorn voice, oft she fell to speaking,  
Calling after the Pilgrim in his wanderings,  
To ask him where her Basil lay, and, shrinking,  
Why it from her was hid: “For cruel ’tis,” quoth she,  
“To bear away my Basil-pot from me.”
source_text:
Piteous she look'd on dead and senseless things,
Asking for her lost Basil amorously;
And with melodious chuckle in the strings
Of her lorn voice, she oftentimes would cry
After the Pilgrim in his wanderings,
To ask him where her Basil was; and why
'Twas hid from her: "For cruel 'tis," said she,
"To steal my Basil-pot away from me."

text:
Time is a test of trouble,
But not a remedy.
If such it prove, it prove too
There was no malady.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text: Defarge came in, breathless, snatched off the red cap he wore, and glanced about him. “Listen, everywhere!” said madame once more. “Listen to him!” Defarge stood there, panting, with eager eyes and open mouths gathered behind him outside the door; and all who were within the wine-shop had risen to their feet.
source_text: Defarge came in breathless, pulled off a red cap he wore, and looked around him! “Listen, everywhere!” said madame again. “Listen to him!” Defarge stood, panting, against a background of eager eyes and open mouths, formed outside the door; all those within the wine-shop had sprung to their feet.

text:
O Liberty! O mate for me!
Here too the blaze, the grape-shot and the axe, in reserve, to fetch
them out in case of need,
Here too, though long represt, can never be destroy’d,
Here too could rise at last murdering and ecstatic,
Here too demanding full arrears of vengeance.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
O Liberty! O companion fit for me!  
Here, too, the blaze, the grape-shot, and the axe, held back in readiness, to bring  
them forth in time of need,  
Here, too, though long suppressed, can never be destroyed,  
Here, too, at last might rise, murderous and ecstatic,  
Here, too, demanding all the debt of vengeance paid.
source_text:
O Liberty! O mate for me!
Here too the blaze, the grape-shot and the axe, in reserve, to fetch
them out in case of need,
Here too, though long represt, can never be destroy’d,
Here too could rise at last murdering and ecstatic,
Here too demanding full arrears of vengeance.

text:
So sailors say, on yesterday,
Just as the dusk was brown,
One little boat gave up its strife,
And gurgled down and down.

text:
Should Hornie, as in ancient days,
’Mang sons o’ God present him,
The vera sight o’ Moodie’s face,
To ’s ain het hame had sent him
Wi’ fright that day.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text: “There!” he said, setting the bundle down on the table. “See to that, and do as much as you can with it. It was trouble enough to get; I had thought I ought to have been here three hours ago.”
source_text: “There!” he said, laying the bundle on the table. “Take care of that, and do the most you can with it. It’s been trouble enough to get; I thought I should have been here, three hours ago.”

text:
A whole long month of May in this sad plight
Made their cheeks paler by the break of June:
"To-morrow will I bow to my delight,
To-morrow will I ask my lady's boon."--
"O may I never see another night,
Lorenzo, if thy lips breathe not love's tune."--
So spake they to their pillows; but, alas,
Honeyless days and days did he let pass;

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
‘Like clouds inwoven in the silent sky,
By winds from distant regions meeting there,
In the high name of truth and liberty,
Around the City millions gathered were,
By hopes which sprang from many a hidden lair,—
Words which the lore of truth in hues of flame
Arrayed, thine own wild songs which in the air
Like homeless odours floated, and the name
Of thee, and many a tongue which thou hadst dipped in flame.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Geography, the world, is in it,
The Great Sea, the brood of islands, Polynesia, the coast beyond,
The coast you henceforth are facing--you Libertad! from your Western
golden shores,
The countries there with their populations, the millions en-masse
are curiously here,
The swarming market-places, the temples with idols ranged along the
sides or at the end, bonze, brahmin, and llama,
Mandarin, farmer, merchant, mechanic, and fisherman,
The singing-girl and the dancing-girl, the ecstatic persons, the
secluded emperors,
Confucius himself, the great poets and heroes, the warriors, the castes,
all,
Trooping up, crowding from all directions, from the Altay mountains,
From Thibet, from the four winding and far-flowing rivers of China,
From the southern peninsulas and the demi-continental islands, from
Malaysia,
These and whatever belongs to them palpable show forth to me, and
are seiz’d by me,
And I am seiz’d by them, and friendlily held by them,
Till as here them all I chant, Libertad! for themselves and for you.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Geography, the world, is in it,  
The great Sea, the brood of isles, Polynesia, the coast beyond,  
The coast thou henceforth art facing--you Libertad! from thy Western  
golden shores,  
The lands there with their peoples, the millions en-masse  
are strangely here,  
The thronging market-places, the temples with idols ranged along the  
sides or at the end, bonze, brahmin, and llama,  
Mandarin, peasant, trader, artificer, and fisher,  
The singing-girl and the dancing-girl, the ecstatic beings, the  
shut-up emperors,  
Confucius himself, the mighty poets and heroes, the warriors, the castes,  
all,  
Marching up, pressing from every quarter, from the Altay mountains,  
From Thibet, from the four winding and far-streaming rivers of China,  
From the southern peninsulas and the half-continental islands, from  
Malaysia,  
These and whatever unto them belongs, palpable, display themselves to me, and  
are seiz’d by me,  
And I am seiz’d by them, and lovingly held by them,  
Till thus here I chant them all, Libertad! for themselves and for you.

text: “Now,” he said, coming back—so it seemed from the sound—to the place where he had stood before, “you have given us most valuable assistance, young woman, and I wish you to profit by it. What can I do to serve you?”
source_text: “Now,” he said, returning: so it seemed by the sound: to the spot where he had stood before, “you have given us most valuable assistance, young woman, and I wish you to be the better for it. What can I do to serve you?”

text:
Reckoning ahead O soul, when thou, the time achiev’d,
The seas all cross’d, weather’d the capes, the voyage done,
Surrounded, copest, frontest God, yieldest, the aim attain’d,
As fill’d with friendship, love complete, the Elder Brother found,
The Younger melts in fondness in his arms.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Reckoning before thee, O soul, when thou, the season won,  
The seas all pass’d, the headlands weather’d, the voyage done,  
Encompass’d, thou dost face and meet God, yielding, the end attain’d,  
As, full of friendship, love made perfect, the Elder Brother found,  
The Younger melts in tenderness within his arms.
source_text:
Reckoning ahead O soul, when thou, the time achiev’d,
The seas all cross’d, weather’d the capes, the voyage done,
Surrounded, copest, frontest God, yieldest, the aim attain’d,
As fill’d with friendship, love complete, the Elder Brother found,
The Younger melts in fondness in his arms.

text:
Auto-da-fe and judgment
Are nothing to the bee;
His separation from his rose
To him seems misery.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Auto-da-fé and judgment  
Are naught to the bee;  
His sundering from his rose  
To him is misery.
source_text:

text:
Years after years,
Through blood, and tears,
And a thick hell of hatreds, and hopes, and fears;
We waded and flew,
And the islets were few
Where the bud-blighted flowers of happiness grew.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
Year after year,
Through blood and tears,
And a deep hell of hatreds, and hopes, and fears;
We waded and flew,
And the isles were but few
Where the bud-blighted blossoms of happiness grew.
source_text:
Years after years,
Through blood, and tears,
And a thick hell of hatreds, and hopes, and fears;
We waded and flew,
And the islets were few
Where the bud-blighted flowers of happiness grew.

text:
O how shall I, unskilfu’, try
The poet’s occupation?
The tunefu’ powers, in happy hours,
That whisper inspiration;
Even they maun dare an effort mair
Than aught they ever gave us,
Ere they rehearse, in equal verse,
The charms o’ lovely Davies.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
O how shall I, unskilfu’, dare  
The poet’s craft and calling?  
The tunefu’ powers, in happy hours,  
That murmur inspiration;  
Even they maun take a nobler flight  
Than aught they e’er have shown us,  
Ere they recite, in equal strain,  
The charms o’ lovely Davies.
source_text:
O how shall I, unskilfu’, try
The poet’s occupation?
The tunefu’ powers, in happy hours,
That whisper inspiration;
Even they maun dare an effort mair
Than aught they ever gave us,
Ere they rehearse, in equal verse,
The charms o’ lovely Davies.

text:
Of scenes of Nature, fields and mountains,
Of skies so beauteous after a storm, and at night the moon so
unearthly bright,
Shining sweetly, shining down, where we dig the trenches and
gather the heaps,
I dream, I dream, I dream.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text: “Begone! I will not listen to you. There can be no fellowship between you and me; we are enemies. Begone, or let us test our strength in combat, in which one of us must fall.”
source_text: “Begone! I will not hear you. There can be no community between you and me; we are enemies. Begone, or let us try our strength in a fight, in which one must fall.”

text:
Flaunt of the sunshine I need not your bask--lie over!
You light surfaces only, I force surfaces and depths also.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Sun’s proud display, I need not your warm lie—move on!
You touch only the face of things; I drive through the skin and the depths as well.
source_text:
Flaunt of the sunshine I need not your bask--lie over!
You light surfaces only, I force surfaces and depths also.

text:
l. 259. Striving . . . itself. Her distrust of her brothers is shown
in her effort not to betray her fears to them.

text:
l. 259. Striving . . . itself.
Her doubt of her brothers is made known
through her endeavor not to give
her fears away to them.

in her effort not to betray her fears to them.

text:
Others will enter the gates of the ferry and cross from shore to shore,
Others will watch the run of the flood-tide,
Others will see the shipping of Manhattan north and west, and the
heights of Brooklyn to the south and east,
Others will see the islands large and small;
Fifty years hence, others will see them as they cross, the sun half
an hour high,
A hundred years hence, or ever so many hundred years hence, others
will see them,
Will enjoy the sunset, the pouring-in of the flood-tide, the
falling-back to the sea of the ebb-tide.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Remorse is cureless, -- the disease
Not even God can heal;
For 't is his institution, --
The complement of hell.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text: “Concerning her—no! I believe she has but just returned home with her husband. They made their arrangements to come back this afternoon—to the inn farther on from here.”
source_text: “About her—no! She has just come home, I believe, with her husband. They arranged to return this afternoon—to the inn beyond here.”

text:
Of ownership--as if one fit to own things could not at pleasure enter
upon all, and incorporate them into himself or herself;
Of vista--suppose some sight in arriere through the formative chaos,
presuming the growth, fulness, life, now attain’d on the journey,
(But I see the road continued, and the journey ever continued;)
Of what was once lacking on earth, and in due time has become
supplied--and of what will yet be supplied,
Because all I see and know I believe to have its main purport in
what will yet be supplied.

text: “My man, don’t be frightened,” said the dealer in red, his manner all at once growing mild. “I had forgotten you were here. It is only a strange way in which reddlemen go out of their minds for a moment; but they do no one any harm. And what did the lady say then?”
source_text: “My man, don’t you be afraid,” said the dealer in red, suddenly becoming gentle. “I forgot you were here. That’s only a curious way reddlemen have of going mad for a moment; but they don’t hurt anybody. And what did the lady say then?”

text:
Apart from the pulling and hauling stands what I am,
Stands amused, complacent, compassionating, idle, unitary,
Looks down, is erect, or bends an arm on an impalpable certain rest,
Looking with side-curved head curious what will come next,
Both in and out of the game and watching and wondering at it.

source_text: “There—Mrs. Coggan is going!” said Bathsheba, exhaling her relief in the form of a long breath which had lain in her bosom a minute or more.

text:
How blest the solitary’s lot,
Who, all-forgetting, all forgot,
Within his humble cell,
The cavern, wild with tangling roots,
Sits o’er his newly gather’d fruits,
Beside his crystal well!
Or haply, to his ev’ning thought,
By unfrequented stream,
The ways of men are distant brought,
A faint, collected dream;
While praising, and raising
His thoughts to heav’n on high,
As wand’ring, meand’ring,
He views the solemn sky.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
How blessed the hermit’s portion,
Who, all forgetting, all forgot,
Within his lowly cell,
The cavern wild with interwoven roots,
Sits o’er his freshly gathered fruits,
Beside his crystal well!

Or, perhaps, to his evening musing,
By some untraveled stream,
The ways of men come drifting in,
A faint and gathered dream;
While praising, and raising
His thoughts to heaven on high,
As, wandering, meandering,
He looks upon the solemn sky.
source_text:

Within his humble cell,
The cavern, wild with tangling roots,
Sits o’er his newly gather’d fruits,
Beside his crystal well!
Or haply, to his ev’ning thought,
By unfrequented stream,
The ways of men are distant brought,
A faint, collected dream;
While praising, and raising

As wand’ring, meand’ring,

text:
Upon the couch the body lay
Wrapped in the depth of slumber:
Its features were fixed and meaningless,
Yet animal life was there,
And every organ yet performed
Its natural functions: ’twas a sight
Of wonder to behold the body and soul.
The self-same lineaments, the same
Marks of identity were there:
Yet, oh, how different! One aspires to Heaven,
Pants for its sempiternal heritage,
And ever-changing, ever-rising still,
Wantons in endless being.
The other, for a time the unwilling sport
Of circumstance and passion, struggles on;
Fleets through its sad duration rapidly:
Then, like an useless and worn-out machine,
Rots, perishes, and passes.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
I dream’d in a dream I saw a city invincible to the attacks of the
whole of the rest of the earth,
I dream’d that was the new city of Friends,
Nothing was greater there than the quality of robust love, it led the rest,
It was seen every hour in the actions of the men of that city,
And in all their looks and words.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
And then she wept aloud, and in her arms
Clasped that bright Shape, less marvellously fair
Than her own human hues and living charms;
Which, as she leaned in passion’s silence there,
Breathed warmth on the cold bosom of the air,
Which seemed to blush and tremble with delight;
The glossy darkness of her streaming hair
Fell o’er that snowy child, and wrapped from sight
The fond and long embrace which did their hearts unite.

text:
O haud your tongue, my feirrie auld wife,
O haud your tongue, now Nansie, O:
I’ve seen the day, and sae hae ye,
Ye wad na ben sae donsie, O.
I’ve seen the day ye butter’d my brose,
And cuddl’d me late and early, O;
But downa-do’s come o’er me now,
And oh, I find it sairly, O!

text:
Then, kneeling down to Heaven’s Eternal King,
The saint, the father, and the husband prays:
Hope “springs exulting on triumphant wing,”
That thus they all shall meet in future days,
There, ever bask in uncreated rays,
No more to sigh, or shed the bitter tear,
Together hymning their Creator’s praise,
In such society, yet still more dear;
While circling Time moves round in an eternal sphere

He wouldn’t find cause for woe.

text:
Forlorn! the very word is like a bell
To toll me back from thee to my sole self!
Adieu! the fancy cannot cheat so well
As she is fam'd to do, deceiving elf.
Adieu! adieu! thy plaintive anthem fades
Past the near meadows, over the still stream,
Up the hill-side; and now 'tis buried deep
In the next valley-glades:
Was it a vision, or a waking dream?
Fled is that music:--Do I wake or sleep?

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Now life’s chilly evening dim shades on your eye,
And ushers the long dreary night:
But you, like the star that athwart gilds the sky,
Your course to the latest is bright.

text:
If solitude hath ever led thy steps
To the shore of the immeasurable sea,
And thou hast lingered there
Until the sun’s broad orb
Seemed resting on the fiery line of ocean,
Thou must have marked the braided webs of gold
That without motion hang
Over the sinking sphere:
Thou must have marked the billowy mountain clouds,
Edged with intolerable radiancy,
Towering like rocks of jet
Above the burning deep:
And yet there is a moment
When the sun’s highest point
Peers like a star o’er ocean’s western edge,
When those far clouds of feathery purple gleam
Like fairy lands girt by some heavenly sea:
Then has thy rapt imagination soared
Where in the midst of all existing things
The temple of the mightiest Daemon stands.

Why Paradise defer,
Why floods are served to us in bowls, --
I speculate no more.

As if some miracle, some hand divine unseal’d my eyes,
Shadowy vast shapes smile through the air and sky,
And on the distant waves sail countless ships,
And anthems in new tongues I hear saluting me.

text:
That auld, capricious carlin, Nature,
To mak amends for scrimpit stature,
She’s turn’d you off, a human creature
On her first plan,
And in her freaks, on ev’ry feature
She’s wrote the Man.

O’ Boreas o’er the pool,
An’ dawin’ it is dreary,
When birks are bare at Yule.

And dawning is but dreary,

text:
Until sweet Isabella's untouch'd cheek
Fell sick within the rose's just domain,
Fell thin as a young mother's, who doth seek
By every lull to cool her infant's pain:
"How ill she is," said he, "I may not speak,
And yet I will, and tell my love all plain:
If looks speak love-laws, I will drink her tears,
And at the least 'twill startle off her cares."

Is it you that thought the President greater than you?
Or the rich better off than you? or the educated wiser than you?

Is it thou that deemed the President mightier than thou?  
Or the wealthy more blest than thou? or the learned wiser than thou?

text:
FIRST SPIRIT:
O thou, who plumed with strong desire
Wouldst float above the earth, beware!
A Shadow tracks thy flight of fire—
Night is coming!
Bright are the regions of the air,
And among the winds and beams
It were delight to wander there—
Night is coming!

Matchless with horse, rifle, song, supper, courtship,

text:
I looked, and lo! one stood forth eloquently.
His eyes were dark and deep, and the clear brow
Which shadowed them was like the morning sky,
The cloudless Heaven of Spring, when in their flow
Through the bright air, the soft winds as they blow
Wake the green world—his gestures did obey
The oracular mind that made his features glow,
And where his curved lips half-open lay,
Passion’s divinest stream had made impetuous way.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
I looked, and lo! one rose before me, eloquent.  
His eyes were dark and deep, and the clear brow  
That overhung them seemed like the morning sky,  
The cloudless Heaven of Spring, when in their flow  
Through the bright air, the gentle winds that blow  
Stir the green world awake—his gestures still obeyed  
The speaking mind that lit his features so,  
And where his curved lips lay half ajar,  
Passion’s most divine tide had forced its ardent way.

Wake the green world—his gestures did obey
The oracular mind that made his features glow,
And where his curved lips half-open lay,
Passion’s divinest stream had made impetuous way.

text:

A Lord—a Peer—an Earl’s son!
Up higher yet, my bonnet
An’ sic a Lord!—lang Scoth ells twa,
Our Peerage he o’erlooks them a’,
As I look o’er my sonnet.

Our Peerage he o’erlooks them a’,
As I o’erlook my sonnet.

To cotton-field drudge or cleaner of privies I lean,
On his right cheek I put the family kiss,
And in my soul I swear I never will deny him.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
There was no food, the corn was trampled down,
The flocks and herds had perished; on the shore
The dead and putrid fish were ever thrown;
The deeps were foodless, and the winds no more
Creaked with the weight of birds, but, as before
Those winged things sprang forth, were void of shade;
The vines and orchards, Autumn’s golden store,
Were burned;—so that the meanest food was weighed
With gold, and Avarice died before the god it made.

text:

Geneva's farthest skill
Can't put the puppet bowing
That just now dangled still.

text:
The domestic joys, the dally housework or business, the building of
houses, are not phantasms, they have weight, form, location,
Farms, profits, crops, markets, wages, government, are none of them
phantasms,
The difference between sin and goodness is no delusion,
The earth is not an echo, man and his life and all the things of his
life are well-consider’d.

text:

Tell how wi’ you, on ragweed nags,
They skim the muirs an’ dizzy crags,
Wi’ wicked speed;

text: Optimization tools, such as SVM-struct (Joachims, 2008), are concerned with parameter estimation. Model libraries, such as CRFSuite (Okazaki, 2007), CRF++ (Kudo, 2005), and NCRF++ (Yang and Zhang, 2018), provide inference for a fixed collection of widely used models, typically linear-chain CRFs. General-purpose inference libraries, such as PyStruct (Müller and Behnke, 2014) or TurboParser (Martins et al., 2010), rely on external solvers for inference, chiefly MAP inference, including integer linear programming solvers and ADMM. Probabilistic programming languages, for instance languages integrated with deep learning such as Pyro (Bingham et al., 2019), enable specification and inference over some discrete domains. More ambitiously, inference libraries such as Dyna (Eisner et al., 2004) support declarative descriptions of dynamic programming algorithms to enable inference for generic algorithms. Torch-Struct adopts a different strategy, integrating a library of optimized structured distributions into a vectorized deep learning framework. We start by motivating this design with a case study.
source_text: Optimization tools, such as SVM-struct (Joachims, 2008) , focus on parameter estimation. Model libraries, such as CRFSuite (Okazaki, 2007) , CRF++ (Kudo, 2005) , or NCRF++ (Yang and Zhang, 2018) , implement inference for a fixed set of popular models, usually linear-chain CRFs. General-purpose inference libraries, such as PyStruct (Müller and Behnke, 2014) or Tur-boParser (Martins et al., 2010), utilize external solvers for (primarily MAP) inference such as integer linear programming solvers and ADMM. Probabilistic programming languages, for example languages that integrate with deep learning such as Pyro (Bingham et al., 2019) , allow for specification and inference over some discrete domains. Most ambitiously, inference libraries such as Dyna (Eisner et al., 2004) allow for declarative specifications of dynamic programming algorithms to support inference for generic algorithms. Torch-Struct takes a different approach and integrates a library of optimized structured distributions into a vectorized deep learning system. We begin by motivating this approach with a case study.

Another way in which SUGILITE exploits GUI groundings in natural language instructions is by inferring task parameters and their possible values. This enables the agent to learn generalized procedures—for example, how to order any type of beverage from Starbucks—from a demonstration of a specific task instance, such as ordering an iced cappuccino. SUGILITE accomplishes this by matching the user utterance (e.g., “order a cup of iced cappuccino”) with the data descriptions of the target UI elements (e.g., click on the menu item that has the text “Iced Cappuccino”) and with the arguments of the demonstrated actions (e.g., put “Iced Cappuccino” into a search box) to find correspondences. Through this process, different parts of the utterance are grounded to specific actions in the demonstrated procedure. It then examines the hierarchical GUI structure at the time of demonstration and searches for alternative GUI elements that are structurally parallel to the original target GUI element. In this manner, it derives other possible values for the identified parameter, such as the names of all the other drinks shown in the same menu as “Iced Cappuccino”.
source_text: Another way SUGILITE leverages GUI groundings in the natural language instructions is to infer task parameters and their possible values. This allows the agent to learn generalized procedures (e.g., to order any kind of beverage from Starbucks) from a demonstration of a specific instance of the task (e.g., ordering an iced cappuccino). SUGILITE achieves this by comparing the user utterance (e.g., "order a cup of iced cappuccino") against the data descriptions of the target UI elements (e.g., click on the menu item that has the text "Iced Cappuccino") and the arguments (e.g., put "Iced Cappuccino" into a search box) of the demonstrated actions for matches. This process grounds different parts in the utterances to specific actions in the demonstrated procedure. It then analyzes the hierarchical structure of GUI at the time of demonstration, and looks for alternative GUI elements that are in parallel to the original target GUI element structurally. In this way, it extracts the other possible values for the identified parameter, such as the names of all the other drinks displayed in the same menu as "Iced Cappuccino"

To handle ambiguous and untranslatable input questions, PHOTON uses a discriminatively trained classifier to identify user inputs for which a SQL mapping cannot be determined immediately. This includes questions that are incomplete (e.g. What is the total?), ambiguous or vague (e.g. Show me homes with good schools), outside the representational scope of SQL (e.g. How many tourists visited all of the 10 attractions?), or simply noisy (e.g. Cyrus teaches physics in department).
source_text: In order to handle ambiguous and untranslatable input questions, PHOTON adopts a discriminatively trained classifier to detect user input to which a SQL mapping cannot be immediately determined. This covers questions that are incomplete (e.g. What is the total?), ambiguous or vague (e.g. Show me homes with good schools), beyond the representation scope of SQL (e.g. How many tourists visited all of the 10 attractions?), or simply noisy (e.g. Cyrus teaches physics in department).

Model architecture: As the name suggests, RoBERTa-DDI relies on pre-trained RoBERTa representations (Liu et al., 2019) to encode the input sequences. For further details on BERT and the transformer architecture, we refer readers to Liu et al. 2019, Devlin et al. (2018), and Vaswani et al. (2017). In RoBERTa-DDI, we attach a dropout layer followed by a single feedforward output layer with a softmax nonlinearity. This layer takes as input the [CLS] token representation from the top transformer layer and produces probabilities over the labels {0, 1}, where 1 denotes an interaction.
source_text: Model architecture: As the name implies, RoBERTa-DDI uses the pre-trained RoBERTa representations (Liu et al., 2019) to encode input sequences. We refer readers to Liu et al. 2019, Devlin et al. (2018) , and Vaswani et al. (2017) for more details on BERT and transformer architecture. For the RoBERTa-DDI model, we add a dropout layer followed by one feedforward (output) layer with a softmax non-linearity, which takes the representation of the [CLS] token at the top transformer layer as input and outputs probabilities for labels {0, 1}, where 1 indicates an interaction.

A particularly valuable application of ITL is in conversational virtual assistants (e.g., Apple Siri, Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa). These systems have been widely adopted by end users to carry out tasks across a range of domains through natural language dialogue. However, a major limitation of such systems is that their task completion and language understanding abilities are restricted to a small set of preprogrammed tasks (Li et al., 2018b). This limited coverage is insufficient for the diverse “long-tail” of user needs and preferences (Li et al., 2017a). Although some software agents expose APIs that allow third-party developers to create new “skills” for them, doing so requires substantial programming expertise and the appropriate APIs, and is therefore inaccessible to the vast majority of end users.
source_text: A particularly useful application of ITL is for conversational virtual assistants (e.g., Apple Siri, Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa). These systems have been widely adopted by end users to perform tasks in a variety of domains through natural language conversation. However, a key limitation of these systems is that their task fulfillment and language understanding capabilities are limited to a small set of pre-programmed tasks (Li et al., 2018b; . This limited support is not adequate for the diverse "long-tail" of user needs and preferences (Li et al., 2017a) . Although some software agents provide APIs to enable thirdparty developers to develop new "skills" for them, this requires significant programming expertise and relevant APIs, and therefore is not usable by the vast majority of end users.

We believe the time is now to develop a unified toolkit that supports research in both E2E and cascaded approaches. We introduce ESPnet-ST, a toolkit that implements many recent models for E2E-ST, as well as the ASR and MT modules for Cascade-ST. Our aim is to provide a toolkit in which researchers can easily incorporate and evaluate new ideas across different approaches. Recent studies suggest that pre-training, multi-task learning, and transfer learning are important techniques for obtaining improved results in E2E-ST (Bérard et al., 2018; Anastasopoulos and Chiang, 2018; Bansal et al., 2019; . Therefore, a unified toolkit that allows researchers to seamlessly mix and match different ASR and MT models when training both E2E-ST and Cascade-ST systems would facilitate research in this area. 1 ESPnet-ST is especially designed for the ST task. ESPnet was originally developed for the 1 There are many excellent toolkits that support both ASR and MT tasks (see Table 1 ). However, it is not always straightforward to use them for E2E-ST and Cascade-ST, because of incompatible training/inference pipelines across modules or the absence of detailed preprocessing/training scripts.
source_text: We believe the time is ripe to develop a unified toolkit that facilitates research in both E2E and cascaded approaches. We present ESPnet-ST, a toolkit that implements many of the recent models for E2E-ST, as well as the ASR and MT modules for Cascade-ST. Our goal is to provide a toolkit where researchers can easily incorporate and test new ideas under different approaches. Recent research suggests that pre-training, multi-task learning, and transfer learning are important techniques for achieving improved results for E2E-ST (Bérard et al., 2018; Anastasopoulos and Chiang, 2018; Bansal et al., 2019; . Thus, a unified toolkit that enables researchers to seamlessly mix-and-match different ASR and MT models in training both E2E-ST and Cascade-ST systems would facilitate research in the field. 1 ESPnet-ST is especially designed to target the ST task. ESPnet was originally developed for the 1 There exist many excellent toolkits that support both ASR and MT tasks (see Table 1 ). However, it is not always straightforward to use them for E2E-ST and Cascade-ST, due to incompatible training/inference pipelines in different modules or lack of detailed preprocessing/training scripts.

text: Model compression and knowledge distillation: Prior works in the vision community dealing with huge architectures like AlexNet and ResNet have addressed this challenge in two ways. Works in model compression use quantization (Gong et al., 2014) , low-precision training and pruning the network, as well as their combination (Han et al., 2016) to reduce the memory footprint. On the other hand, works in knowledge distillation leverage student teacher models. These approaches include using soft logits as targets (Ba and Caruana, 2014) , increasing the temperature of the softmax to match that of the teacher (Hinton et al., 2015) as well as using teacher representations (Romero et al., 2015 ) (refer to (Cheng et al., 2017 ) for a survey). of the teacher models which adds architectural constraints (e.g., embedding dimension) on the student. In order to address this shortcoming, more recent works combine task-specific distillation with pre-training the student model with arbitrary embedding dimension but still relying on transformer architectures (Turc et al., 2019) ; (Jiao et al., 2019) ; (Zhao et al., 2019) . Izsak et al. (2019) ; Shi et al. (2019) extend these for sequence tagging for Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and Named Entity Recognition (NER) in English. The one closest to our work Tsai et al. (2019) extends the above for multilingual NER.

metadata.title: XtremeDistil: Multi-stage Distillation for Massive Multilingual Models

text: Model compression and knowledge distillation: Earlier work in the vision community on large architectures such as AlexNet and ResNet has tackled this problem in two main ways. Studies on model compression apply quantization (Gong et al., 2014), low-precision training, and network pruning, as well as combinations of these methods (Han et al., 2016), to shrink the memory footprint. In contrast, knowledge distillation methods rely on student–teacher models. These methods include treating soft logits as supervision targets (Ba and Caruana, 2014), raising the softmax temperature to align it with the teacher’s output (Hinton et al., 2015), and using teacher representations (Romero et al., 2015) (see Cheng et al., 2017 for a survey), which imposes architectural constraints on the student, such as the embedding dimension. To mitigate this limitation, more recent work has combined task-specific distillation with pre-training the student model at an arbitrary embedding dimension, while still depending on transformer architectures (Turc et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). Izsak et al. (2019) and Shi et al. (2019) extend these ideas to sequence tagging for Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and Named Entity Recognition (NER) in English. The work most closely related to ours is Tsai et al. (2019), which extends the same line of research to multilingual NER.
source_text: Model compression and knowledge distillation: Prior works in the vision community dealing with huge architectures like AlexNet and ResNet have addressed this challenge in two ways. Works in model compression use quantization (Gong et al., 2014) , low-precision training and pruning the network, as well as their combination (Han et al., 2016) to reduce the memory footprint. On the other hand, works in knowledge distillation leverage student teacher models. These approaches include using soft logits as targets (Ba and Caruana, 2014) , increasing the temperature of the softmax to match that of the teacher (Hinton et al., 2015) as well as using teacher representations (Romero et al., 2015 ) (refer to (Cheng et al., 2017 ) for a survey). of the teacher models which adds architectural constraints (e.g., embedding dimension) on the student. In order to address this shortcoming, more recent works combine task-specific distillation with pre-training the student model with arbitrary embedding dimension but still relying on transformer architectures (Turc et al., 2019) ; (Jiao et al., 2019) ; (Zhao et al., 2019) . Izsak et al. (2019) ; Shi et al. (2019) extend these for sequence tagging for Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and Named Entity Recognition (NER) in English. The one closest to our work Tsai et al. (2019) extends the above for multilingual NER.

text: Cross-Domain Datasets. In this setting, we investigate three NLP tasks: POS tagging, NER and text classification. For POS tagging task, we use the GUM portion (Zeldes, 2017) (Nguyen et al., 2017) 72.30 * 61.17 * 66.27 * ---HMM-SLM (Nguyen et al., 2017) 76.19 * 66.24 * 70.87 * ---MTL-MVT (Wang et al., 2018) 81 domains: academic, bio, fiction, news, voyage, wiki, and interview. For NER task, we select the English portion of the OntoNotes v5 corpus (Hovy et al., 2006) . The corpus is annotated with 9 named entities with data from 6 domains: broadcast conversation (bc), broadcast news (bn), magazine (mz), newswire (nw), pivot text (pt), telephone conversation (tc), and web (web). Multi-Domain Sentiment Dataset (MDS) v2.0 (Blitzer et al., 2007) is used for text classification, which is built on Amazon reviews from 4 domains: books, dvd, electronics, and kitchen. Since the dataset only contains word frequencies for each review without raw texts, we follow the setting in Chen and Cardie (2018) considering 5,000 most frequent words and use the raw counts as the feature vector for each review.

metadata.title: Learning to Contextually Aggregate Multi-Source Supervision for Sequence Labeling

text: Modern ASR systems rely on end-to-end, alignment free neural architectures, i.e. CTC (Graves et al., 2006) or sequence to sequence models (Graves et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2017) . The use of attention mechanisms significantly improve results in (Chorowski et al., 2015) and (Chan et al., 2016) . Recently, the success of transformer architectures for NLP tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019) has motivated speech researchers to investigate their efficacy in end-to-end ASR (Karita et al., 2019b) . Zhou et. al., apply an end-to-end transformer architecture for Mandarin Chinese ASR (Zhou et al., 2018) . Speech-Transformer extends the scaled dot-product attention mechanism to 2D and achieves competitive results for character level recognition Karita et al., 2019a) . Pham et. al. introduce the idea of stochastically deactivating layers dur-ing training to achieve a very deep model (Pham et al., 2019) . A major challenge of the transformer architecture is the quadratic memory complexity as a function of the input sequence length. Most architectures employ consecutive feature stacking (Pham et al., 2019) or CNN preprocessing Karita et al., 2019b) to downsample input feature vectors. Mohamed et al. (2019) use a VGG-based input network to downsample the input sequence and achieve learnable positional embeddings.

metadata.title: Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers

text: Modern ASR systems are based on end-to-end, alignment-free neural architectures, namely CTC (Graves et al., 2006) and sequence-to-sequence models (Graves et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2017). The incorporation of attention mechanisms substantially improves performance in (Chorowski et al., 2015) and (Chan et al., 2016). More recently, the strong performance of transformer architectures on NLP tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019) has led speech researchers to examine their effectiveness for end-to-end ASR (Karita et al., 2019b). Zhou et al. apply an end-to-end transformer architecture to Mandarin Chinese ASR (Zhou et al., 2018). Speech-Transformer extends scaled dot-product attention to two dimensions and attains competitive results for character-level recognition (Karita et al., 2019a). Pham et al. introduce stochastic layer deactivation during training in order to build a very deep model (Pham et al., 2019). A key limitation of the transformer architecture is its quadratic memory complexity with respect to input sequence length. Most architectures therefore use consecutive feature stacking (Pham et al., 2019) or CNN preprocessing (Karita et al., 2019b) to downsample input feature vectors. Mohamed et al. (2019) employ a VGG-based input network to downsample the input sequence and obtain learnable positional embeddings.

text: We conduct experiments on image captioning (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , investigating benefits brought by the Guider network. In image captioning, instead of using a discriminator to define final rewards for generated sentence, we adopt evaluation metrics computed based on human references. The final rewards appear more important as they contain reference (ground-truth) information. Feature-matching rewards work as a regularizer of the final rewards. We call our model in this setting a guider-matching sequence training (GMST) model. An overview of GMST is provided in the Appendix. We test our proposed model on the MS COCO dataset (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , containing 123,287 images in total. Each image is annotated with at least 5 captions. Following Karpathy's split (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , 5,000 images are used for both validation and testing. We report BLEU-k (k from 1 to 4), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015) , and ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) scores. We consider two settings: (i) using a pre-trained 152layer ResNet (He et al., 2016) for feature extraction, where we take the output of the 2048-way pool5 layer from ResNet-152, pretrained on the ImageNet dataset; and (ii) using semantic tags detected from the image as features . We use an LSTM with 512 hidden units with mini-batches of size 64. Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) is used for optimization, with learning rate 2 × 10 −4 . We pretrain the captioning model for the maximum 20 epochs, then use the reinforcement learning to train it for 20 epochs and test on the best model on the validation set.

metadata.title: Improving Adversarial Text Generation by Modeling the Distant Future

text: We perform experiments on image captioning (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) to examine the gains introduced by the Guider network. In the image captioning task, rather than employing a discriminator to specify the final rewards for the generated sentence, we use evaluation metrics computed from human references. These final rewards are seemingly more influential, since they incorporate reference, i.e., ground-truth, information. The feature-matching rewards serve as a regularizer for the final rewards. In this setting, we refer to our model as a guider-matching sequence training (GMST) model. An overview of GMST is given in the Appendix. We evaluate the proposed model on the MS COCO dataset (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015), which contains 123,287 images in total. Each image is annotated with at least five captions. Following Karpathy's split (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015), 5,000 images are used for validation and another 5,000 for testing. We report BLEU-k scores (k ranging from 1 to 4), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015), and ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005). We consider two configurations: (i) using a pre-trained 152-layer ResNet (He et al., 2016) for feature extraction, where we take the output of the 2048-dimensional pool5 layer from ResNet-152 pretrained on ImageNet; and (ii) using semantic tags detected from the image as features. We use an LSTM with 512 hidden units and mini-batches of size 64. For optimization, we employ Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) with a learning rate of 2 × 10−4. We first pretrain the captioning model for up to 20 epochs, then train it with reinforcement learning for 20 additional epochs, and finally test the best model on the validation set.

text: Context-Aware Text Generation. There are some related works on context-aware text generation (Mikolov and Zweig, 2012; Tang et al., 2016; Mangrulkar et al., 2018) . Most previous works on language modeling with contextual information (Wang and Cho, 2016; Wang et al., 2018; Sordoni et al., 2015b; Wen et al., 2015; Vinyals and Le, 2015) treat the preceding sentences as context. Compared with these sequential generation tasks, our task is constrained by bidirectional context, and is more challenging. Text infilling (Zhu et al., 2019) aims at filling in the missing part, given the rest of a sentence. proposes an iterative inference algorithm based on gradient search for text infilling. For story infilling, (Ippolito et al., 2019) first predicts rare words in the missing span, and then generates text conditioned on these words. SpanBERT (Joshi et al., 2020) masks random contiguous spans and (pre-)trains a language model to predict tokens in the span. XL-Editor (Shih et al., 2019) adapts XL-Net to text infilling and other editing tasks. (Kang and Hovy, 2019) models logic connections between sentences and generates intermediate sentences grounded on inter-sentential "flow." (Bhagavatula et al., 2020) formulates abductive commonsense reasoning as a natural language inference task to decide the appropriate reason that could explain the observation in one sentence given the background described by another sentence. proposes a text style transfer task to translate a sentence in the context of a paragraph into the desired style. These three works study generation tasks that address inter-sentential relationship, and thus may be conceptually related to our motivation.

metadata.title: INSET: Sentence Infilling with INter-SEntential Transformer

text: Structure-based relational reasoning. Structural information has been widely used for relational reasoning in various NLP applications including question answering (Dhingra et al., 2018; De Cao et al., 2019; Song et al., 2018a) and relation extraction (Sahu et al., 2019; . Song et al. (2018a) and (De Cao et al., 2019) leverage co-reference information and set of rules to construct document-level entity graph. GCNs (Kipf and Welling, 2017) or GRNs (Song et al., 2018b) are applied to perform reasoning for multi-hop question answering (Welbl et al., 2018) . Sahu et al. (2019) also utilize co-reference links to construct the dependency graph and use labelled edge GCNs (Marcheggiani and Titov, 2017) for document-level relation extraction. Instead of using GNNs, use the edgeoriented model (Christopoulou et al., 2018) for logical inference based on a heterogeneous graph constructed by heuristics. Unlike previous approaches that use syntactic trees, co-references or heuristics, LSR model treats the document-level structure as a latent variable and induces it in an iteratively refined fashion, allowing the model to dynamically construct the graph for better relational reasoning.

metadata.title: Reasoning with Latent Structure Refinement for Document-Level Relation Extraction

text: Baselines. We compare ExpBERT against several baselines that train a classifier over the same input representation u(x). NoExp trains a classifier only on u(x). The other baselines augment u(x) with variants of the explanation representation v(x). BERT+SemParser uses the semantic parser from Hancock et al. (2018) to convert explanations into executable logical forms. The resulting denotations over the input x (a single bit for each explanation) are used as the explanation representation, i.e., v(x) ∈ {0, 1} n . We use two different sets of explanations for this baseline: our natural language explanations (LangExp) and the low-level explanations from Hancock et al. (2018) that are more suitable for the semantic parser (ProgExp). BERT+Patterns converts explanations into a collection of unigram, bigram, and trigram patterns and creates a binary feature for each pattern based on whether it is contained in s or not. This gives v(x) ∈ {0, 1} n , where n is the number of patterns. Finally, we compare ExpBERT against a

metadata.title: ExpBERT: Representation Engineering with Natural Language Explanations

text: In this paper, we investigated the difference in accuracy between sequence labeling performed on clean and noisy text ( §2.3). We formulated the noisy sequence labeling problem ( §2.2) and introduced a model that can be used to estimate the real noise distribution ( §3.1). We developed the noise induction procedure that simulates the real noisy input ( §3.2). We proposed two noise-aware training methods that boost sequence labeling accuracy on the perturbed text: (i) Our data augmentation approach uses a mixture of clean and noisy examples during training to make the model resistant to erroneous input ( §3.3). (ii) Our stability training algorithm encourages output similarity for the original and the perturbed input, which helps the model to build a noise invariant latent representation ( §3.4). Our experiments confirmed that NAT consistently improved efficiency of popular sequence labeling models on data perturbed with different error distributions, preserving accuracy on the original input ( §4). Moreover, we avoided expensive re-training of embeddings on noisy data sources by employing existing text representations. We conclude that NAT makes existing models applicable beyond the idealized scenarios. It may support an automatic correction method that uses recognized entity types to narrow the list of feasible correction candidates. Another application is data anonymization (Mamede et al., 2016) .

metadata.title: NAT: Noise-Aware Training for Robust Neural Sequence Labeling

As in automatic evaluations, ResNet152 features performed more poorly. The retrieval model outperformed the generative model, a result that has been observed in other (text-only) dialogue tasks (Dinan et al., 2019; . In turn 1, TRANSRESNET RET (ResNeXt-IG-3.5B) has a win rate against humans of 49.4% (difference not significant using a binomial two-tailed test, p > 0.5), while both other models are significantly outperformed by humans (p < 2 × 10 −7 compared to ResNet152 features), showing the importance of our retrieval architecture and image feature choices. We thus compare only TRANSRESNET RET (ResNeXt-IG-3.5B) to humans in all three turns (Fig. 4, right) . That model performs well, with an overall win rate against humans of 47.7% (difference is significant, p < 7 × 10 −5 ). text: Step 3 score(r) = score(z)+score(r a )+score(r b ) R.push(r, score(r)) end for end for return R major steps: Figure 3 shows the overall workflow for one iteration (here, the root node of the sentence is selected for illustration). First, we select sub-phrase pairs of the input phrase that respect parse-tree boundaries, where each pair consists of non-overlapping phrases (Step 1). Since the aim is to learn generic syntax-governed rearrangements, we abstract out the two sub-phrases, and replace them with non-terminal symbols, retaining only the constituent tag information. For example, we show three phrase pairs in Figure 3 that can be abstracted away to yield the reduced forms of the sentences. We then use a seq2seq model to obtain rearrangements for each abstracted phrase (Step 2). Finally, this top-level rearrangement is combined with recursively-constructed phrase rearrangements within the abstracted phrases to obtain sentence-level rearrangements (Step 3).

text: Currently, neural generation techniques have powered many inspiring applications, e.g., poem generation , neural machine translation (NMT) (Bahdanau et al., 2015) and chatbot (Zhao et al., 2017) . Conditional (also known as controllable) text generation is an important task of text generation, aiming to generate realistic text that carries a specific attribute (e.g., positive or negative sentiment). A common solution is to encode the condition into a vector representation and then integrate it with the text generation process Hu et al., 2017; Mirza and Osindero, 2014) . These existing neural models have achieved encouraging results. However, when a new condition is added (e.g., a new topic for categorical generation), they require a full retraining or finetuning. This process is both time-consuming and computationally inefficient (Houlsby et al., 2019) . Both fine-tuning and retraining are not desirable in real-world applications since the delivery (e.g., transmitting updated weights through the Internet) and client-side re-deployment (e.g., distribute updated weights to users) of large-scale weights are often difficult.

metadata.title: Pre-train and Plug-in: Flexible Conditional Text Generation with Variational Auto-Encoders

text: At present, neural generation methods have enabled many compelling applications, such as poem generation, neural machine translation (NMT) (Bahdanau et al., 2015), and chatbots (Zhao et al., 2017). Conditional, also termed controllable, text generation is a significant subtask of text generation, whose goal is to produce realistic text with a specified attribute (for example, positive or negative sentiment). A widely used approach is to encode the condition as a vector representation and incorporate it into the text generation procedure (Hu et al., 2017; Mirza and Osindero, 2014). These neural models have yielded promising performance. However, when a new condition is introduced, such as a new topic in categorical generation, they must be fully retrained or fine-tuned. This is both time-consuming and computationally inefficient (Houlsby et al., 2019). In practical applications, neither fine-tuning nor retraining is ideal, because distributing updated large-scale weights, such as through the Internet, and redeploying them on the client side, for instance by delivering the updated weights to users, are often difficult.

This process is both time-consuming and computationally inefficient (Houlsby et al., 2019) . Both fine-tuning and retraining are not desirable in real-world applications since the delivery (e.g., transmitting updated weights through the Internet) and client-side re-deployment (e.g., distribute updated weights to users) of large-scale weights are often difficult.

text: Previous works on DST use different approaches, ranging from using handcrafted features to elicit utterance information Wang and Lemon, 2013) . use Convolutional Neural Networks to learn utterance representations. However, their models do not scale as they do not share parameters across different slots. Zhong et al. (2018) and Nouri and Hosseini-Asl (2018) propose a new global module that shares information to facilitate knowledge transfer. However, they rely on a predefined ontology. Xu and Hu (2018) use a pointer network with a Seq2Seq architecture to handle unseen slot values. use a pre-trained BERT model (Devlin et al., 2019) to encode slots and utterances and uses multi-head attention (Vaswani et al., 2017) to find relevant information in the dialogue context for predicting slot values. Wu et al. (2019) introduce an encoder-decoder architecture with a copy mechanism, sharing all model parameters between all domains. Zhou and Small (2019) formulate multidomain DST as a question answering task and use reading comprehension techniques to generate the answers by either span or value prediction. Johnson et al. (2017b) propose single encoderdecoder models for zero-shot machine translation by encoding language and input sentence jointly, and Zhao and Eskenazi (2018) propose cross-domain zero-shot language generation using a cross-domain embedding space.

metadata.title: Zero-Shot Transfer Learning with Synthesized Data for Multi-Domain Dialogue State Tracking

text: Previous work on DST has adopted a range of approaches, from handcrafted features designed to extract utterance information (Wang and Lemon, 2013) to Convolutional Neural Networks for learning utterance representations. However, these models do not scale well because they do not share parameters across different slots. Zhong et al. (2018) and Nouri and Hosseini-Asl (2018) propose a global module that shares information to support knowledge transfer, but their methods depend on a predefined ontology. Xu and Hu (2018) employ a pointer network within a Seq2Seq architecture to address unseen slot values. They use a pre-trained BERT model (Devlin et al., 2019) to encode slots and utterances, and apply multi-head attention (Vaswani et al., 2017) to identify relevant information in the dialogue context for slot-value prediction. Wu et al. (2019) introduce an encoder-decoder architecture with a copy mechanism, sharing all model parameters across all domains. Zhou and Small (2019) cast multidomain DST as a question answering task and apply reading comprehension techniques to produce answers through either span prediction or value prediction. Johnson et al. (2017b) propose single encoder-decoder models for zero-shot machine translation by jointly encoding the language and the input sentence, and Zhao and Eskenazi (2018) propose cross-domain zero-shot language generation using a cross-domain embedding space.

text: Recently, work has started to leverage paragraphlevel contexts to produce better questions. Du and Cardie (2018) incorporated coreference knowledge to better encode entity connections across documents. Zhao et al. (2018) applied a gated selfattention mechanism to encode contextual information. However, in practice, semantic structure is difficult to distil solely via self-attention over the entire document. Moreover, despite considering longer contexts, these works are trained and evaluated on SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016 ), which we argue as insufficient to evaluate deep QG because more than 80% of its questions are shallow and only relevant to information confined to a single sentence (Du et al., 2017 Figure 2 : The framework of our proposed model (on the right) together with an input example (on the left). The model consists of four parts: (1) a document encoder to encode the input document, (2) a semantic graph encoder to embed the document-level semantic graph via Att-GGNN, (3) a content selector to select relevant question-worthy contents from the semantic graph, and (4) a question decoder to generate question from the semantic-enriched document representation. The left figure shows an input example and its semantic graph. Dark-colored nodes in the semantic graph are question-worthy nodes that are labeled to train the content selection task.

metadata.title: MobileBERT: a Compact Task-Agnostic BERT for Resource-Limited Devices

text: Inspired by the recent success of Variational Auto-Encoder (VAE) (Kingma and Welling, 2014) based post-hoc conditional image generation strategy (Engel et al., 2018) , we provide a new perspective for flexible conditional text generation. We propose Pre-train and Plug-in Variational Auto-Encoder (PPVAE), which decouples the text generation module from the condition representation module. PPVAE is a hierarchical framework composed of two VAEs: (1) PRETRAINVAE, which derives the global latent space of text with its encoder (pre-trained global encoder) and learns to generate text based on an easily-accessible large unlabeled dataset with its decoder (pre-trained global decoder); (2) PLUGINVAE, which is a lightweight neural network that learns to transform vectors from the conditional latent space to the global latent space, and vice versa. This mapping function can be easily learned with only a few conditional training samples. In this sense, once we transform a latent variable (also known as latent code) randomly sampled from the conditional space distribution to the global space, the pre-trained global decoder is directly adopted for generation. In other words, whenever a new condition emerges, we only need to train a PLUGINVAE and directly plug it into the framework.

metadata.title: Pre-train and Plug-in: Flexible Conditional Text Generation with Variational Auto-Encoders

text: SpanBERT show about the same error rate on the groups per:loc and same nertag&positive. They differ, however, in which examples they predict correctly: For per:loc, 78.6% are predicted by both models, and 21.4% are predicted by only one of the models. For same nertag&positive, 12.8% of the examples are predicted by only of the models. The two models thus seem to identify complementary information. One difference between the models is that KnowBERT has access to entity information, while SpanBERT masks entity spans. To test how much the two models balance context and argument information, we apply rewriting to alter the instances belonging to a group and observe the impact on performance. We use two strategies: (1) we remove all tokens outside the span between head and tail argument (outside), and (2) we remove all tokens between the two arguments (between). We find that SpanBERT's performance on per:loc drops from 62.1 F1 to 57.7 (outside) and 43.3 (between), whereas Know-BERT's score decreases from 63.7 F1 to 60.9 and 50.1, respectively. On same nertag&positive, we observe a drop from 89.2 F1 to 58.2 (outside) and 47.7 (between) for SpanBERT. Know-BERT achieves a score of 89.4, which drops to 83.8 and 49.0. The larger drop in performance on same nertag&positive suggests that SpanBERT, which uses entity masking, focuses more on the context, whereas KnowBERT focuses on the entity content because the model has access to the arguments. Surprisingly, both models show similar performance on the full test set (Table 5 ). This suggests that combining both approaches may further improve RE performance.

text: We now explain how to inject knowledge about narrative structure into our summarization models. For both models, such knowledge is transferred via a network pre-trained on the TRIPOD 4 dataset introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019) . This dataset contains 99 movies annotated with turning points. TPs are key events in a narrative that define the progression of the plot and occur between consecutive acts (thematic units). It is often assumed (Cutting, 2016) that there are six acts in a film (Figure 1 ), each delineated by a turning point (arrows in the figure). Each of the five TPs has also a well-defined function in the narrative: we present each TP alongside with its definition as stated in screenwriting theory (Hauge, 2017 ) and adopted by Papalampidi et al. (2019) in Table 1 (see Appendix A for a more detailed description of narrative structure theory). Papalampidi et al. (2019) identify scenes in movies that correspond to these key events as a means for analyzing the narrative 2019structure of movies. They collect sentence-level TP annotations for plot synopses and subsequently project them via distant supervision onto screenplays, thereby creating silver-standard labels. We utilize this silver-standard dataset in order to pretrain a network which performs TP identification.

metadata.title: Screenplay Summarization Using Latent Narrative Structure

text: We now describe how knowledge of narrative structure is incorporated into our summarization models. For both models, this knowledge is conveyed through a network pre-trained on the TRIPOD 4 dataset introduced by Papalampidi et al. (2019). This dataset comprises 99 movies annotated with turning points. TPs are pivotal narrative events that shape plot progression and are situated between successive acts, or thematic units. It is commonly assumed (Cutting, 2016) that a film consists of six acts (Figure 1), each separated by a turning point (shown as arrows in the figure). Moreover, each of the five TPs has a specific narrative role: we list each TP together with its definition as given in screenwriting theory (Hauge, 2017) and adopted by Papalampidi et al. (2019) in Table 1 (see Appendix A for a more detailed account of narrative structure theory). Papalampidi et al. (2019) detect movie scenes that align with these key events as a way to analyze the narrative structure of films. They first gather sentence-level TP annotations for plot synopses and then project them onto screenplays using distant supervision, producing silver-standard labels. We use this silver-standard dataset to pretrain a network for TP identification.
