text: To answer the second question, we carry out two analyses: (1) we conduct a manual explorative analysis of model misclassifications on the most challenging test instances and categorize them into several linguistically motivated error categories; (2) we formulate these categories into testable hypotheses, which we can automatically validate on the full test set by adversarial rewriting -removing the suspected cause of error and observing the change in model prediction (Wu et al., 2019) . We find that two groups of ambiguous relations are responsible for most of the remaining errors. The dataset also contains clues that are exploited by models without entity masking, e.g. to correctly classify relations even with limited access to the sentential context.

text: To address the second question, we conduct two analyses: (1) we perform a manual exploratory examination of model misclassifications on the most difficult test instances and group them into several linguistically motivated error categories; and (2) we translate these categories into testable hypotheses, which we then automatically evaluate on the full test set through adversarial rewriting—namely, by removing the suspected source of error and examining the resulting change in model prediction (Wu et al., 2019). We find that two classes of ambiguous relations account for most of the remaining errors. The dataset also includes cues that models without entity masking can exploit, for example, to classify relations correctly even when access to the sentential context is limited.

Results are shown in Table 8 . Overall, all neural text generation systems are getting better in generating repetition-free and coherent single-sentence summaries of news articles. Transformer-based models, TRANS2S and BERTS2S in particular, perform superior to RNNbased PTGEN and CNN-based TCONVS2S models. Nonetheless, Table 9 shows that these metrics fail to correlate with faithful, hallucinated and factual assessments of summaries. Fleiss's Kappa (k) values for repetition and incoherence assessments showed almost a perfect agreement (0.81 ≤ k ≤ 1.0; Landis and Koch, 1977) among our raters (see Table 6 ). Table 10 has the full results from our human study of hallucinations.

text: 2) Image based features: Since the word likelihood values received from language models are in essence signals, we explore signal detection approaches as well. For example, for audio classifi-cation (Hershey et al., 2017) store plots of the logmel spectogram of the audios as images and then apply image classification methods. VGG (Simonyan and Zisserman, 2014), was one of the top performers of the different classifiers they tested. Inspired by them, we explore obfuscation detection via image classification. Specifically, we explore a transfer learning approach wherein we use the VGG-19 classifier 4 trained for image classification on ImageNet dataset 5 . For our method, we sort the extracted likelihood values for the text in descending order and then plot these values saving it as an image. This image is then processed by the pre-trained VGG-19. We extract the document's 6 representation from the last flatten layer of VGG-19 (before the fully connected layers) as it contains high-level information regarding edges and patterns in the image. We expect this resulting feature representation vector to capture information regarding text smoothness.

text: Context and relevance. Learning to label sequences of text is one of the more thoroughly explored topics in natural language processing. In recent times, neural networks are applied not only to sequential labeling like part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) or named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016) , but also to cast into a labeling framework otherwise non-sequential tasks such as syntactic parsing (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019) .

metadata.title: MultiQT: Multimodal Learning for Real-Time Question Tracking in Speech

text: Context and significance. Learning to assign labels to text sequences is among the most extensively studied problems in natural language processing. More recently, neural networks have been used not only for sequential labeling tasks such as part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) and named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016), but also to reformulate inherently non-sequential tasks, such as syntactic parsing, within a labeling framework (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019).

text: We conduct experiments on the WebNLG dataset (Gardent et al., 2017; Castro Ferreira et al., 2018) used in the WebNLG challenge. 5 For each instance, the input is a set of up to 7 RDF triples from DBPedia, and the output is their text descriptions. Each triple-set is paired with a set of (up to three) humangenerated reference texts. Each reference is also paired with the order of triples it realized. We use them to train and evaluate our Planner. Overall, the dataset contains 9, 674 unique triple-sets and 25, 298 text references, and is divided into training, development, and test set. The test set contains two subsets, the SEEN part where the instances belong to one of the nine domains that are seen in the training and development set (such as Astronaut and Food), and the UNSEEN part where the instances are from the other five unseen domains. The UNSEEN part is designed to evaluate models' generalizability to out-of-domain instances.

text: We perform experiments on the WebNLG dataset (Gardent et al., 2017; Castro Ferreira et al., 2018) employed in the WebNLG challenge. 5 For each example, the input consists of a set of up to seven RDF triples from DBPedia, and the target is a textual description of those triples. Each triple set is associated with up to three human-written reference texts. In addition, each reference is annotated with the order in which the triples were realized, and we use these annotations to train and evaluate our Planner. In total, the dataset comprises 9,674 distinct triple sets and 25,298 text references, and it is split into training, development, and test sets. The test set contains two subsets: the SEEN portion, in which the instances come from one of the nine domains observed in the training and development sets (e.g., Astronaut and Food), and the UNSEEN portion, in which the instances are drawn from the other five unseen domains. The UNSEEN portion is intended to assess models’ ability to generalize to out-of-domain instances.

text: Retrieve-and-edit text generation methods have received significant recent interest; editing humanauthored text can potentially avoid many of the challenges that are seen while generating text from scratch, including the tendency to be overly repetitive or to degrade on longer texts (Holtzman et al., 2018 (Holtzman et al., , 2019 . Retrieve-and-edit methods have been developed for summarization , machine translation (Wu et al., 2019) , language modeling , and conversation generation (Weston et al., 2018) . These methods first retrieve a single output from the training set, and then use a learned model to edit it into the final output.

text: Identifying BABY-STEPs Our learning approach requires an agent to follow microinstructions (i.e., the BABY-STEPs). Existing datasets (Anderson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019) do not provide fine-grained segmentations of long instructions. Therefore, we use a template matching approach to aggregate consecutive sentences into BABY-STEPs. First, we extract the noun phrase using POS tagging. Then, we employs heuristic rules to chunk a long instruction into shorter segments according to punctuation and landmark phrase (i.e., words for concrete objects). We document the details in the Appendix.

metadata.title: BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps

text: Identifying BABY-STEPs Our learning framework requires an agent to execute microinstructions (i.e., BABY-STEPs). However, existing datasets (Anderson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019) do not offer fine-grained segmentations of lengthy instructions. We therefore adopt a template-matching strategy to combine adjacent sentences into BABY-STEPs. First, we extract the noun phrase via POS tagging. We then apply heuristic rules to divide a long instruction into shorter segments based on punctuation and landmark phrases (i.e., words denoting concrete objects). We provide the full details in the Appendix.

For each condition, we use a condition-specific PLUGINVAE to derive the conditional space. That is, PLUGINVAE is proposed to learn the transformation between the conditional and global latent space for each condition. Specifically, for each condition c i , we use a limited number of conditional samples y i and utilize the global encoder enc g to encode them into v y i . Note that normally, the encoded text samples under a single condition are not likely to densely clustered in the global text space Z g , since the learning process of Z g is condition-independent and the unlabeled corpus contains diverse text samples. text: Effect of the OT constraint loss We further compare the performance of the model (a) without using OT loss, (b) with using the whole table and text to compute OT, and (c) with using the extracted nouns from both table and text to compute OT. Results are presented in the third, fifth, and sixth rows of Table 3 , respectively. The model with the OT loss improve performance on almost all scores, especially on the PARENT-T score. text: • With the prior hierarchy knowledge, we adopt typical structure encoders for modeling label dependencies in both top-down and bottomup manners, which has not been investigated for hierarchical text classification. • We propose a novel end-to-end hierarchyaware global model (HiAGM). We further present two variants for label-wise text features, a hierarchy-aware multi-label attention model (HiAGM-LA) and a hierarchy-aware text feature propagation model (HiAGM-TP). • We empirically demonstrate that both variants of HiAGM achieve consistent improvements on various datasets when using different structure encoders. Our best model outperforms the state-of-the-art model by 3.25% of Macro-F1 and 0.66% of Micro-F1 on RCV1-V2.

metadata.title: Hierarchy-Aware Global Model for Hierarchical Text Classification

text: • Leveraging prior knowledge of the hierarchy, we employ conventional structure encoders to model label dependencies in both top-down and bottom-up directions, a setting that has not yet been explored for hierarchical text classification. • We introduce a novel end-to-end hierarchy-aware global model (HiAGM). In addition, we propose two variants for label-wise text features: a hierarchy-aware multi-label attention model (HiAGM-LA) and a hierarchy-aware text feature propagation model (HiAGM-TP). • Experiments show that both HiAGM variants yield consistent gains across multiple datasets when paired with different structure encoders. Our strongest model surpasses the state of the art by 3.25% in Macro-F1 and 0.66% in Micro-F1 on RCV1-V2.
source_text: • With the prior hierarchy knowledge, we adopt typical structure encoders for modeling label dependencies in both top-down and bottomup manners, which has not been investigated for hierarchical text classification. • We propose a novel end-to-end hierarchyaware global model (HiAGM). We further present two variants for label-wise text features, a hierarchy-aware multi-label attention model (HiAGM-LA) and a hierarchy-aware text feature propagation model (HiAGM-TP). • We empirically demonstrate that both variants of HiAGM achieve consistent improvements on various datasets when using different structure encoders. Our best model outperforms the state-of-the-art model by 3.25% of Macro-F1 and 0.66% of Micro-F1 on RCV1-V2.

text: Hierarchy-Aware Multi-Label Attention The first variant of HiAGM is proposed based on multilabel attention, called as HiAGM-LA. Attention mechanism is usually utilized as the memory unit in text classification (Yang et al., 2016; Du et al., 2019) . Recent LCL studies (Huang et al., 2019; You et al., 2019) construct one multi-label attentionbased model per level so as to avoid optimizing label embedding among different levels.

metadata.title: Hierarchy-Aware Global Model for Hierarchical Text Classification

text: Span Extraction for Dialog. We have recently witnessed increasing interest in intent-restricted approaches (Coucke et al., 2018; Goo et al., 2018; for slot-filling. In this line of work, slot-filling is treated as a span extraction problem where slots are defined to occur only with certain intents. This solves the issue of complex categorical modeling but makes slot-filling dependent on an intent detector. Therefore, we propose a framework that treats slot-filling as a fully intentagnostic span extraction problem. Instead of using rules to constrain the co-occurrence of slots and intents, we identify a slot as either a single span of text or entirely absent. This makes our approach more flexible than prior work; it is fully independent of other system components. Regardless, we can explicitly capture turn-by-turn context by adding an input feature denoting whether a slot was requested for this dialog turn (see Figure 1 ).

metadata.title: Span-ConveRT: Few-shot Span Extraction for Dialog with Pretrained Conversational Representations

text: Models. Since our proposed methodology is agnostic towards the chosen word emotion model, we will re-use models from the literature. In particular, we will rely on the multi-task learning feed-forward network (MTLFFN) worked out by Buechel and Hahn (2018b) . This network constitutes the current state of the art for monolingual emotion lexicon creation (expanding an existing lexicon for a given language) for many of the datasets in Table 2 .

text: All of the above datasets are said to be "opendomain", as the corpus is Wikipedia. There are also datasets for specialized domains. The biomedical QA dataset (Tsatsaronis et al., 2015) contained 29 development questions (arguably too few for training an automated system) and 282 test questions, divided into four categories-'yes/no', factoid, list, and summary. InsuranceQA (Ins), a dataset for the insurance industry, is a corpus for intent detection, rather than for MRC.

text: All of the aforementioned datasets are described as “open-domain,” since the underlying corpus is Wikipedia. There are likewise datasets for specialized domains. The biomedical QA dataset (Tsatsaronis et al., 2015) included 29 development questions—arguably too few to train an automated system—and 282 test questions, organized into four categories: “yes/no,” factoid, list, and summary. InsuranceQA (Ins), a dataset for the insurance sector, is a corpus for intent detection rather than for MRC.

text: (1) BERT-Classification and (2) BERT-Regression fine tune the pre-trained BERT to perform a 5-class classification and regression respectively directly using the noisy self-reported ratings. To test BERT-Classification on dialog pairs, we apply the DEX trick (Rothe et al., 2015) to get a floating-point number of predicted rating and thus get rid of the cases when the model predicts the dialog pairs as tie.

text: Hyperparameters We use the uncased BERT-Base (Devlin et al., 2019) to calculate contextual embeddings for all models. We use ADAM (Kingma and Ba, 2015) to train the models with batch size 4 and a learning rate of 1e-5. For the CRF framework, we learn the scaling parameter λ during training, which is important to get stable results. For L-TapNet, we set α as 0.5 and β as 0.7. We fine-tune BERT with Gradual Unfreezing trick (Howard and Ruder, 2018) . text: PERSONA-CHAT has fueled a growing interest in developing methods for personalized dialogue generation. Mazaré et al. (2018) incorporated additional data from Reddit to train the model. Wolf et al. (2019b) fine-tuned pretrained language model (Radford et al., 2018) to improve the dialogue generation. Although both works demonstrate promising results, they focus more on mimicking the style of human-like responses, leaving understudied the aspects of explicitly modeling understanding between interlocutors. Our work, instead, takes the perspective of understanding modeling.

metadata.title: You Impress Me: Dialogue Generation via Mutual Persona Perception

text: 1. The two versions of our model -P1 and P2 -consistently outperform all other baselines in BLEU. Specifically, our model with DP-based semantic graph (P2) achieves an absolute improvement of 2.05 in BLEU-4 (+15.2%), compared to the document-level QG model which employs gated self-attention and has been enhanced with the same decoder as ours (B5). This shows the significant effect of semantic-enriched document representations, equipped with auxiliary content selection for generating deep questions.

metadata.title: Semantic Graphs for Generating Deep Questions

text: AdaptaBERT This baseline corresponds to a state-of-the-art unsupervised domain adaptation approach (AdaptaBERT) (Han and Eisenstein, 2019) . The approach first uses unlabeled data from both the source and target domains to domain-tune a pretrained BERT model. The model is finally tasktuned in a supervised fashion on the source domain labelled data (Ontonotes). At inference time, the model makes use of the pretraining and domain tuning to predict entities in the target domain. In our experiments, we use the cased-version of the base BERT model and perform three fine-tuning epochs for both domain-tuning and task-tuning. We additionally include an ensemble model, which averages the predictions of five BERT models finetuned with different random seeds.

metadata.title: Named Entity Recognition without Labelled Data: A Weak Supervision Approach

text: AT as illustrated in Figure 1 (a) actually tries to model the sentences of both languages in the target language view with partial source words replaced with the target words and the full target language sentence. Bi-view AT enhances AT by adding another language view. Figure 1 (b) adds the source language view shown in the right part to accompany with the target language view of Figure 1 (a) . In particular, the target language sentence "t 1 t 2 t 3 t 4 t 5 " is in the form of "t 1 t 2 t 3 s t 4 t 5 s " after looking up the bilingual dictionary. Such partial target words replaced with the source words and the full source language sentence "s 1 s 2 s 3 s 4 " constitute the source language view.

text:
We danced and leapt the whole long day,
Till even the piper lads were tired and weary;
Yet Charlie still must bear the cost
For kissing Theniel’s bonnie Mary.
Theniel Menzies’ bonnie Mary, &c.
source_text:
We lap a’ danc’d the lee-lang day,
Till piper lads were wae and weary;
But Charlie gat the spring to pay
For kissin Theniel’s bonie Mary.
Theniel Menzies’ bonie Mary, &c.

text:
And the spell of the carol seized me,
As though I were held by my comrades’ hands in the night,
And the voice of my spirit kept time with the bird’s song.
source_text:
And the charm of the carol rapt me,
As I held as if by their hands my comrades in the night,
And the voice of my spirit tallied the song of the bird.

text:
Months have slipped by in lingering circles,
Each night the specter makes its way,
With trembling tread it stirs the ground,
With trembling tread it wanders near me still—
source_text:
Months are passed in lingering round,
Every night the spectre comes,
With thrilling step it shakes the ground,
With thrilling step it round me roams—

text:
Before you had a thought of spring,
save as a guess,
you saw, God bless his quick arrival,
a figure in the sky
in colors all his own,
a little weatherworn,
with garments full of life
in indigo and brown.
source_text:
Before you thought of spring,
Except as a surmise,
You see, God bless his suddenness,
A fellow in the skies
Of independent hues,
A little weather-worn,
Inspiriting habiliments
Of indigo and brown.

text:
My hands, my limbs grow numb and lifeless,  
My brain is torn and lost in turmoil,  
Though the ancient beams should split apart, I will not let go,  
I will hold thee close, O God, though the waves beat hard against me,  
Thee, thee at least I know.
source_text:
My hands, my limbs grow nerveless,
My brain feels rack’d, bewilder’d,
Let the old timbers part, I will not part,
I will cling fast to Thee, O God, though the waves buffet me,
Thee, Thee at least I know.

text:
Then Mercury replied with careful speech:—
“You have asked wisely what my art can do;
I grudge you nothing that I know to teach,
not even now—for in both word and will
I would be mild with you; in your wise soul
you can attain all things, and your place
is highest in Heaven among the sons of Jove,
who loves you with the fullness of his love.”
source_text:
To whom thus Mercury with prudent speech:—
‘Wisely hast thou inquired of my skill:
I envy thee no thing I know to teach
Even this day:—for both in word and will
I would be gentle with thee; thou canst reach
All things in thy wise spirit, and thy sill
Is highest in Heaven among the sons of Jove,
Who loves thee in the fulness of his love.

text:
Fasten the threads to my life, my Lord,
Then I am ready to leave!
Only a glance at the horses—
Swift! That will suffice!
source_text:
Tie the strings to my life, my Lord,
Then I am ready to go!
Just a look at the horses --
Rapid! That will do!

text:
I wondered how it would shine in gold  
When Richard rode to mill;  
And then I longed to slip away,  
But some force gripped my will.
source_text:
I thought how yellow it would look
When Richard went to mill;
And then I wanted to get out,
But something held my will.

text:
He hears the lambs’ unguarded cry,  
And the ewes’ soft answer in return;  
He keeps watch while they rest in peace,  
For they know their shepherd has come near.
source_text:
For he hears the lambs’ innocent call,
And he hears the ewes’ tender reply;
He is watchful while they are in peace,
For they know when their shepherd is nigh.

text:
The monks, in wonder, stand and shiver still,  
They break through the chancel’s shadowed gloom;  
From St. Edmond’s shrine, behold—a skeleton hand  
Points toward the black marble tomb.
source_text:
The Monks in amazement shuddering stand,
They burst through the chancel’s gloom,
From St. Edmond’s shrine, lo! a skeleton’s hand,
Points to the black marble tomb.

text:
An ancient tower rose up in thought,
And showed how Dettingen’s bold hero fought;
Yet, far from ever sinking into nought,
It still supports
A lord who in the western climes has fought
With his trusty sword.
source_text:
An ancient tower to memory brought
How Dettingen’s bold hero fought;
Still, far from sinking into nought,
It owns a lord
Who far in western climates fought,
With trusty sword.

text:
Sharp as the arrows  
Of that silver orb,  
Whose fierce beam thins  
In the clear white dawn  
Until we can scarcely see it—we only feel it there.
source_text:
Keen as are the arrows
Of that silver sphere,
Whose intense lamp narrows
In the white dawn clear
Until we hardly see—we feel that it is there.

text:
Farewell, my rhyme-making brother!
Too long have we been strangers to each other:
Now let us rest our minds together,
In brotherly devotion;
Let envy thrash about in tether,
That black infernal notion!
source_text:
Fareweel, “my rhyme-composing” brither!
We’ve been owre lang unkenn’d to ither:
Now let us lay our heads thegither,
In love fraternal:
May envy wallop in a tether,
Black fiend, infernal!

text:
Poet Willie, poet Willie, fire a volley at the Doctor,  
with your “Liberty’s Chain” and all your wit;  
you never swung a leg across Pegasus’ flank,  
you only caught the stench where he had sh—t.  
Poet Willie, you only caught the stench where he had sh—t.
source_text:
Poet Willie! poet Willie, gie the Doctor a volley,
Wi’ your “Liberty’s Chain” and your wit;
O’er Pegasus’ side ye ne’er laid a stride,
Ye but smelt, man, the place where he sh—t.
Poet Willie! Ye but smelt man, the place where he sh—t.

text:
This dust was once the man,  
Kind, plain, just, and firm, beneath whose careful hand  
Against the foulest crime ever known in any land or age,  
The Union of these States was preserved.
source_text:
This dust was once the man,
Gentle, plain, just and resolute, under whose cautious hand,
Against the foulest crime in history known in any land or age,
Was saved the Union of these States.

text:
Her eyes were open, yet she still surveyed,  
Fully awake, the shapes her slumber made:  
A sorrowful change had almost driven out  
The dear delights her pure and deep dream knew throughout.  
Then fair Madeline began to weep,  
And, sighing many times, to murmur words asleep;  
Still fixed upon Porphyro was her sight;  
Who knelt there, hands together, with an eye of plight,  
Dreading to stir or speak, she seemed so strangely bright.
source_text:
Her eyes were open, but she still beheld,
Now wide awake, the vision of her sleep:
There was a painful change, that nigh expell'd
The blisses of her dream so pure and deep
At which fair Madeline began to weep,
And moan forth witless words with many a sigh;
While still her gaze on Porphyro would keep;
Who knelt, with joined hands and piteous eye,
Fearing to move or speak, she look'd so dreamingly.

text:
And I have dreamed that the aim and heart of the life we know,  
the passing,  
is to shape and determine the self of the life unseen, the lasting.
source_text:
And I have dream’d that the purpose and essence of the known life,
the transient,
Is to form and decide identity for the unknown life, the permanent.

text:
Hail, Poetry, shy nymph apart!  
In chasing you, how many have gone astray  
from common sense, or slowly worn themselves thin  
amid the heaps of idle talk;  
and oh, how often your own joys have starved  
even as you kept on giving all your favors!
source_text:
Hail, Poesie! thou Nymph reserv’d!
In chase o’ thee, what crowds hae swerv’d
Frae common sense, or sunk enerv’d
’Mang heaps o’ clavers:
And och! o’er aft thy joes hae starv’d,
’Mid a’ thy favours!

text:
Stroke the cool brow, so oft made burning,
Lift, if you can, the languid hair;
Feel the hard and stubborn fingers—
No thimble more shall ever wear.
source_text:
Stroke the cool forehead, hot so often,
Lift, if you can, the listless hair;
Handle the adamantine fingers
Never a thimble more shall wear.
